text: FOXTON AND SHOTTON, a township in the p. of Sedgefield, co. of Durham, 7 m. WNW of Stockton-upon-Tees, near the Clarence railway. Area 1,720 acres. Pop. 44.
FOY, a parish in Herefordshire, 3 m. N of Ross, on the Wye. Area 2,500 acres. Pop. 278.

FOY (SAINTE), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Gironde, arrond. of Libourne. The cant. comprises 15 com. Pop. in 1831, 10,611; in 1841, 10,789.—The town is 27 m. ESE of Libourne, on the l. bank of the Dordogne. Pop. in 1841, 2,924. It has a Calvinist consistory church. It was formerly fortified, and has sustained several sieges. Wine the produce of the locality, brandy, and corn, form the chief articles of trade.—Also a town of Sardinia, in the div. of Savoy, prov. of Tarentaise, mandemento and 5 m. ESE of St. Maurice, and 19 m. ENE of Moutiers, on the r. bank of the Isère. Pop. 1,100.

FOY-L'ARGENTIE'RE (SAINTE), a village of France, in the dep. of the Rhône, cant. and 2 m. SE of St.-Laurent-de-Chamousset, near the r. bank of the Brevenne. Pop. 600. The environs afford excellent wine, and contain some coal-mines.
FOY-BOISSEILLES, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur, dep. of Foy-Notre-Dame. Pop. 68.

FOY-LES-LYONS (SAINTE), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Rhône, cant. of Genis- Laval, 2 m. SSW of Lyons, near the r. bank of the Rhône. Pop. in 1841, 2,905. It has some ironworks, and produces good wine.
FOY-NOTRE-DAME, a department and commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur. Pop. of dep., 163; of com., 95.
FOY-DE-PEYROLLIE'RES (SAINTE), a town of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Garonne, cant. of St. Lys, 18 m. SW of Toulouse. Pop. 890.

FOYA-DE-MONCHIQUE (LA), a mountain of Portugal, on the N confines of the prov. of Algarva, 30 m. NNE of Cape St. Vincent. It is of granitic formation, and rises to the height of 4,050 ft. above sea-level. Its summit is visible at sea at the distance of 78 m.
FOYE-MONJAULT (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Deux-Sevres, cant. of Beauvoirsur-Niort. Pop. 1,009.

FOYLE, a river of the cos. Donegal, Tyrone, and Londonderry. It is formed by the confluence of the Finn and the Mourne, at the town of Lifford. The tract through which it flows, from Lifford downward, is in general a low plain, prevailingly rich and populous, and partially overlooked by upland pastures; in the vicinity of Londonderry, it puts on a dress of much beauty, and becomes pleasingly picturesque. The river is all tidal; and, owing to the vast aggregate mountain area of the territorial basins drained by the rivers and affluents which form it, its volume of water is large, its current liable to occasional sudden accelerations, and its march along the low country which it traverses solemn and majestic. Its width is aggregately great for an Irish river; and, in the vicinity of Londonderry, varies from 300 yds. to nearly half-a-mile. It is navigable to the bridge of Londonderry for vessels of 600 tons; and to Castle-Finn, 41 m. above the point at which it takes the name of F., for small craft or lighters.—The average annual produce of the salmon-fishery of the F. from 1823 till 1836, was 140 tons.—The F.'s tributaries are the Roe and the Fanghan, both flowing into the estuary of the F.; the Dermot, falling into the Foyle, 6 m. above the bridge of Derry; the Mourne, joining the Foyle above Lifford-bridge; the Derg, rising in the co. of Done- | Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, 13 m. SSW of Lamego. FRA Strabane; the Mournebeg, joining the Derg 2 m. above Castle-Derg.

FOYLE (Lough), the estuary of the river Foyle, between co. Donegal and co. Londonderry. It has nearly a triangular outline. Its most expanded part is at its head; and its most contracted part is at its entrance,—the latter being compressed to a strait considerably less than 1 m. wide. The lough has a very noble appearance at high water. Its W side alone is navigable, and even this is encumbered with shoals; its E side is a flat strand, fringed by a sandy beach, which continues round Magilligan-point on to the mouth of the Bann.

FOYNES, a village, an island, and a harbour, in the p. of Robertstown, co. Limerick. The village is situated on the shore of the Shannon, 21 m. N of Shanagolden. The island lies N and S across the Shannon, from a point about 350 yds. from the village; it has a somewhat oval form, and measures about 2½ m. in circumf. The harbour consists of the sound that separates the island and the mainland, in which there is an ample depth of water at all times of tide.

FOZ, a town of Spain, in Galicia, in the prov. and 45 m. NE of Lugo, partido and 14 m. NE of Mondonedo, on the Atlantic, at the mouth of the Masma, which forms a small port. Pop. 1,063.—Also a village of Portugal, in the prov. of Alemtejo, comarca of Avis, at the confluence of the Zatas and Tagus, 32 m. NE of Lisbon. It has extensive iron-works.
FOZCALADA, a town of Spain, in Aragon, in the prov. of Teruel, partido of Castellote-y-su-Barrio - Montijo, near the confluence of the Calanda with the Guadaloupe.
FOZZA, a village of Austrian Lombardy, in the prov. and 26 m. N of Vicenza. Pop. 1,975.
FRABOSA, a small town of the Sardinian states, in the prov. and 7 m. S of Mondovi. In the vicinity is a quarry of excellent black marble.

FRADE, an elevated summit in the Organ range, in the Brazilian prov. of Rio-de-Janeiro, in the district of Mambucaba, between Angra-dos-Reis and Parati.—Also a serra which separates the prov. of Rio-Grande-do-Norte from that of Ceara.—Also a river in the prov. of Bahia, which flows into the Atlantic 12 m. S of Porto-Seguro.
FRADES (Dos), an island of Brazil, in the bay of Todos-os-Santos, 2 m. N of Itaparica.
FRADING, a market-town of Moravia, in the circle of Znaym.
FRADLEY, a hamlet in the p. of Alrewas, in Staffordshire, 4½ m. NE of Lichfield. Pop. in 1831, 382; in 1841, 362.
FRADSWELL, a chapelry in the p. of Colwich, Stafford, 7½ m. ESE of Stone. Area 1,410 acres. Pop. in 1831, 199; in 1841, 237.

FRAGA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 55 m. SE of Huesca, on the borders of Catalonia. Pop. 5,028. It stands on two hills on the l. bank of the Cinca. The streets are narrow, and the houses are mean. It was formerly a place of strength; at present its chief ornament is its quay, built on the side of the Cinca, which is crossed near this by a wooden bridge of 22 arches. The environs produce excellent figs. This town occupies the site of the Gallica Flavia of the Romans.
FRAGNETO, a town of Naples, in Principato- Ultra, 17 m. WNW of Ariano, near the l. bank of the Tamaro. Pop. 2,500.
FRAGNETO-L'ABBATE, a town of Naples, in Principato-Ultra, 13 m. to the N of the former. Pop. 2,000.
FRAGOA-DE-SAN-PEDRO, a small town of
FRAGOAS, a small town of Portugal, in Estremadura, 6 m. NW of Santaren.
FRAGRANT (UPPER and LOWER), two villages of Carinthia, in the circle of Villach. Copper is wrought in their vicinity.

FRAGUA, a river of the prov. of San-Juan-delos-Llanos, in New Granada, which rises in the range dividing the head-streams of the Magdalena from chose of the Caqueta, and flowing SE enters the Caqueta at Sotano.
FRAHIER, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Saone, cant. of Champagny. Pop. 1,437.
FRAIGNE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Charente, cant. of Aigre. Pop. 1,092.
FRAILS, rocks on the S coast of Ireland, lying off Wexford, 12 m. SW of Carnsore point, in N lat. 52° 6'.
FRAIMBAULT-DE-PRIERES (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. and cant. of Mayenne. Pop. 1,111.
FRAIMBAULT-SUR-PISSE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Orne, cant. of Passais, on the r. bank of the Pisse. Pop. 3,225.
FRAIN, a small town of Moravia, in the gov. of Brunn, on the l. bank of the Theya, 12 m. WNW of Znaym. Pop. 840. It has celebrated porcelain manufactories.
FRAISANS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Jura, cant. of Dampierre, 14 m. NE of Dole. Pop. 485.
FRAISSE, a town of France, in the dep. of Herault, cant. of La Salvetat. Pop. 1,590.
FRAISTHORPE, a parish in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. SSW of Bridlington. Pop., with Auburn, in 1831, 103; in 1841, 92.

FRAIZE, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Vosges, arrond. of Saint-Dié.—The cant., comprising 10 com., had a pop. of 16,839 in 1841.— The com. is 8 m. SSE of Saint-Die, on the r. bank of the Meurthe. Pop. 2,501.
FRAMECOURT, a village of French Flanders, 3 m. S by W of St. Pol, and 20 m. W of Arras.
FRAMENTEAU, a village of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, arrond. of Maulde. Pop. 400.
FRAMERIES, a commune and town of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, arrond. and 3 m. SW of Mons. Pop. 7,944. Coal is largely wrought in the environs.
FRAMERSHEIM, a large village of Hesse-Darmstadt, in the district of Alzey, with 1,000 inhabitants.
FRAMFIELD, a parish of Sussex, 2 m. SE of Uckfield. Area 6,830 acres. Pop. in 1831, 1,468; in 1841, 1,434.
FRAMINGHAM, a township in Middlesex co., in the state of Massachusetts, U. S., 18 m. WSW of Boston. Pop. 3,030.

FRAMLINGHAM, a market-town and parish in Suffolk, 18 m. NNE of Ipswich, and 87 m. NE of London. Area of p. 4,470 acres. Pop. in 1801, 1,854; in 1831, 2,445; in 1841, 2,523.—The town stands on an eminence not far from the sources of the Ore, a river which, passing at a short distance from the town, falls into the sea at Oreford. It is large and tolerably well-built; has a spacious marketplace; and lays claim to a very high antiquity.
FRAMLINGHAM (EARL), a parish in Norfolk, 5 m. SE of Norwich. Area 560 acres. Pop. in 1831, 74; in 1841, 100.
FRAMLINGHAM (PIGOT), a parish in Norfolk, 51 m. SE of Norwich. Area 350 acres. Pop. in 1831, 302; in 1841, 289.
FRAMLINGTON, a parish in Northumberland, 11 m. NW of Morpeth. Area 5,730 acres. Pop. in 1831, 735; in 1841, 702.
FRAMMERSBACH, a village of Bavaria, in the | Gallia Cisalpina. FRA circle of Lower Franconia, in the principality of Aschaffenburg. Pop. 2,188.
FRAMONT, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Luxemburg, arrond. of Anloy. Pop. 224.
FRAMPOL, a village of Poland, in the gov. of Lublin, obwod of Zamosé.

FRAMPTON, a parish in Dorset, 5 m. NW by W of Dorchester, on the NE bank of the Frame, over which there is here a fine bridge. Area 2,080 acres. Pop. in 1831, 376; in 1841, 391.—Also a tything in the p. of Sapperton, Gloucestershire, 64 m. NW of Cirencester. Pop. in 1831, 178.—Also a parish in the co. of Lincoln, 31 m. S of Boston. Area 6,200 acres. Pop. in 1831, 706; in 1841, 784.

FRAMPTON-COTTERELL, a parish in the co. of Gloucester, 4 m. W by S of Chipping-Sodbury, on a branch of the Avon. It includes the tything of Wick-Wick. Area 1,190 acres. Pop. in 1831, 1,816; in 1841, 1,991.

FRAMPTON-UPON-SEVERN, a parish in the co. of Gloucester, 61 m. NNW of Dursley, on the E bank of the Severn. Area 2,360 acres. Pop. in 1831, 1,055; in 1841, 1,051. This p. is frequently inundated by the impetuous tides of the Severn.
FRAMSDEN, a parish in Suffolk, 3 m. SE of Debenham. Area 2,160 acres. Pop. in 1831, 642; in 1841, 829.
FRANCA, or VILLA-FRANCA-DO-IMPERADOR, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of São-Paulo, on the r. bank of the Mugi. Pop. 5,000.
FRANCAIS (CAPE), a headland on the N coast of Hayti, in N lat. 19° 40½, W long. 69° 55'.
FRANCAISE (LA), a town of France, in the dep. of Tarn-et-Garonne, 11 m. N by W of Montauban. Pop. 3,888; of cant., comprising 4 com., 6,394.
FRANCASTEL, a small town of France, in the dep. of the Oise, 14 m. NE of Beauvais.

FRANCAVILLA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Otranto, 22 m. WSW of Brindisi. Pop. in 1838, 11,108. It is large and regularly built; the streets are wide and straight, and the houses showy, though in a heavy style of architecture. A great part of the town having been thrown down by an earthquake in 1734, the houses since erected are only one story high. Many of the inhabitants are employed in manufacturing cotton and tobacco produced in the neighbourhood; olive oil also is made in large quantities.— Also a town of Sicily, in the Val-di-Demona, near the l. bank of the Cantara, 12 m. WNW of Taormina. Pop. 4,000. Silver, copper, lead, and antimony, are mined in the vicinity. Here the Imperialists, under Count Merci, obtained a victory over the Spaniards, under the Marquis de Leyda, in 1719. —Also a town of Naples, in the Basilicata, 20 m. N by W of Cassano. Pop. 1,900.—Also a town of Naples, in Abruzzo-Citra, 3 m. SE of Pescara. Pop. 3,000.—Also a town of Naples, in Calabria-Citra, 9 m. E of Castrovillari. Pop. 1,900.—Also a small town of the Sardinian states, 6 m. S of Novi.

FRANCE. One of the principal states of Continental Europe.— The country situated between the Jura, the Alps, the Mediterranean, the Pyrenees, the Atlantic, the British channel, the German ocean, and the course of the Rhine, did not receive the name of France till after Clovis's conquest. It was anciently named Gaul, from a Celtic word signifying 'a wood,' or 'woody country:' for Gaul was formerly covered with thick forests. The Romans gave it the appellation of Gallia Transalpina, to distinguish it from the countries of Piedmont and Lombardy, which, lying on the S side of the Alps, were known by the name of Boundaries.] F., as it is bounded at present, is defined as one of the separate states of Europe by natural limits on three of its sides. It has the Channel, which separates it from England on the N; the bay of Biscay on the W; and the Mediterranean, with the Pyrenean mountains and the Bidassoa, separating it from Spain on the S. Its inland boundaries, on the NE, E, and SE, have not been traced by the hand of Nature, but are dependent on political circumstances. At present the boundary-line commences to the NE of Dunkirk, betwixt it and Furnes on the coast of W. Flanders. Thence it runs SE, along the ancient limits of the Belgian provs., the late bishopric of Liege, and the duchy of Bouillon. Passing onwards, it separates the grand-duchy of Luxemburg from the dep. of the Ardennes; and thence runs in a SE direction, cutting the Maese, and crossing the Moselle at Syrk, where it leaves the frontier of Belgium, and begins to separate the Prussian dominions from France, running along the W bank of the Saare, between Sarrelouis and Buzonville, and Sarrebruck and Sarreguemines. Thence it passes SE, cutting the mountains of Vosges, as far as the source of the Lauter, along whose S bank it runs till it falls into the Rhine. The frontier-line then runs due S, along the thalweg or course of the Rhine, separating the grand duchy of Baden from France, till it reaches Huningue, where that river becomes navigable. It then turns to the W; after which it runs straight S, and cuts the Vosges, then E, and then S, along the crest of the Jura, separating France from Switzerland on the E, as far as to the S of Geneva, whence it runs S along the Rhone, to that point where the river turns to the W; traversing the Isère at the point where that river becomes navigable, it reaches the Cottian Alps, along which it runs in a SE and then a S direction, separating France from the Sardinian states, as far as the shore of the Mediterranean. Thus the present inland boundaries of France are Belgium on the NE; Germany and Switzerland on the E; and the Sardinian states of Savoy and Piedmont on the SE. Outline and area.] The present republic of F. forms a part of Western Europe, and lies within the temperate zone. Its utmost extent from N to S is 8½°: viz., from the frontiers of Roussillon, in 42½° N lat., to Dunkirk, in 51° 2' N lat., or 590 m. Its greatest length from E to W, or from the mouth of the Lauter, 6° E of the meridian of Paris, to the most western point of the peninsula of Bretagne, 7° W of the same meridian, is 590 m.; and if we take it from Neu Brissac on the Rhine, in N lat. 48° 5', and 7° 40' E long. of Greenwich, to Cape Ushant, in 48° 22' N lat., and 5° 4' W of the same meridian, the length will be the same. Were it not for the peninsula of Bretagne, which stretches 100 m. farther into the Atlantic than any other part of France, its form would almost resemble an oblong square. Or we may regard it as an hexagonal figure, of which the angles are as follows: viz., on the N, Dunkirk; on the NE, the confluence of the Lauter and the Rhine; SE, the embouchure of the Var; S, Cape Cerbere at the E extremity of the Pyrenees; SE, the embouchure of the Bidassoa; and on the E, Point Saint-Mathieu at the entrance of Brest roads. The area of F. has been variously estimated. According to the early map of the Royal Academy of Sciences, it contains 28,386 sq. leagues, of 25 to a degree; according to the map of Delisle, 25,839 such sq. leagues. Nolin's map makes it 28,054; Defer's, 31,278; Sanson's, 31,657. The medium of all these is 28,642. Busching, reckoning 15 m. to a degree, estimated the extent of F., at 10,000 sq. m.; the Marshal de Vauban made it 30,000 sq. leagues, or 140,940,000 arpens; Voltaire, 130,000,000 arpens; Templemann gave it | ties, which were all re-united by Charlemagne, the founder of an extent of 138,837 sq. geog. m., or 119,224,874 acres. Panclon, by covering his map with shot to every indenture of outline, estimated the kingdom to contain 103,021,840 arpens of 100 perches, at 22 ft. the perch, or 1,344\frac{4}{9} toises square to the arpen; but the arpen of Paris contains only 900 toises. According to this measurement, France contains only 81,687,016 English acres; but this is undoubtedly far short of the truth. The Encyclopedie assigns to the area of F. 100,000,000 arpens; observing that, by the map of Cassini, the amount is 125,000,000. The author of L'Impot abonne calculates it at 105,000,000; and the author of Apologie sur l'Edit de Nantes at 135,600,000. All the foregoing calculations are evidently mere approximations founded on varying and uncertain data. M. Neckar seems to have been the first who ascertained the area of F. with considerable accuracy. According to him, F .- not including Corsica—comprehends 26,951 sq. leagues of 2,282 toises, or 156,024,213 arpens of Paris, which is equivalent to 131,722,295 English acres. This calculation was nearly adopted by M. Jorse, author of the Credit Nationale, who reckons that France contains 27,000 leagues, of 5,785 arpens of Paris to a league, or 156,225,720 arpens. The committee of the first National Assembly stated it soon afterwards at 26,463 sq. leagues. Turning to more recent authorities we find the superficial area of F. estimated in the Almanac royal for 1819, at 52,562,300 hectares, or 26,281.15 sq. leagues of 25 to a degree; by Baron C. Dupin—whose calculations are founded on the data of the recent trigonometrical survey—at 53,533,426 hectares, or 132,694,000 acres; by Chaptal; in 1818, at 52,000,000 hectares, or about 128,500,810 acres; in the Tableau Synoptique du Royaume de France, at 52,889,672 hectares, or 130,624,000 acres, or 204,000 sq. m., by Balbi at 205,000 sq. m.; and by the Société de Géographes of Paris at 27,000 sq. leagues. Progressive Geography.—(1). Roman Period.] This country anciently comprehended not only that territory which is now denominated France, but also the duchy of Savoy,-the cantons of Switzerland, then called Helvetia,—the four late electorates of the Rhine: viz., the Palatinate, and the archbishoprics of Mentz, Treves, and Cologne,—and the territories of Liege, Luxemburg, Flanders, and Brabant. Julius Cæsar found Gallia divided into three parts, which he distinguished as G. Belgica, G. Aquitania, and G. Propria or Celtica. The Aquitani had passed, it is supposed, from Spain. They were of African origin, and had seized upon modern Gascony and Bearn; while the warlike German tribes, under the name of Belgae, had settled themselves in the NE parts of Gaul, and introduced the Gothic language and manners. Celtic Gaul, which was inhabited by a people differing in their customs and language from the Belgæ and Aquitani, called in their own language Celts, but by the Romans Galli, was further divided into G. Comata, so called because the people wore long hair,—G. Braccata, in which the people wore breeches,—and G. Togata, where the Roman toga, or gown, had been adopted by the inhabitants after their subjection to the Roman sway. Celtic Gaul comprehended all the territory bounded by the ocean, the Seine, the Marne, the Saone, the Rhine, and the Garonne. Belgic Gaul was bounded by the Seine, the Marne, the mountains of Vosges, the Rhine, and the sea. Aquitania lay between the ocean, the Pyrenees, and the Garonne. Gaul was afterwards divided by Augustus into 4 provinces: viz. G. Narbonensis, G. Aquitania, G. Lugdunensis, and G. Belgica. In the 4th cent. the Notitia Imperii exhibits Gaul divided into 5 provs.; and finally it was divided by Constantine the Great into 17 provs., 6 of which were consular, and 11 under presidents who resided in the capital cities. (2). Under the Franks.] The modern name of F. is derived from the Franks, a German tribe, or rather, as has been supposed, a motley multitude of different tribes, who, uniting themselves in defence of their liberty against the Romans, styled themselves, Franks,—that word signifying in their language, as it still does in ours, free. They inhabited that part of Germany which lies between the Rhine, the Weser, and the German ocean; and were known in the time of Tacitus by the names of Chauci, Cherusci, Catti, &c. In the 5th cent., in conjunction with the Alemans, the Vandals, the Alans, and the Burgundians, the Franks crossed the Rhine, and, under Clovis, achieved the conquest of Gaul. Upon the death of Clovis, the Frank dominion was split into two divisions: viz., Oesterric, or the Eastern kingdom, corruptly called Austrasia,—and Westerric, or the Western kingdom, called Neustria. These were again subdivided into smaller principalithe Carlovingian dynasty; but, under the reign of his weak successors, F. was again split into a number of feudatory principalities, though the name continued paramount through all the struggles of those little monarchies, till at last it became that of the whole kingdom. (3). Under the Capetian dynasty.] Hugh Capet, the founder of the present race of sovereigns, who succeeded the last of the Carlovingian line, in 987, possessed nothing of F. but Picardy, the Isle-of-France, and Orleannais. Berry was reunited to the crown in 1100. Touraine and Normandy were reconquered by Philip Augustus in 1200. Languedoc was annexed in 1316. The final and permanent union of Champagne to the French crown was effected in 1361. The Lyonnais came under the possession of the crown in the reign of Philip the Fair; and Dauphiné, under Philip of Valois, who received it from its last count, on condition that the eldest son of the French king should always take the title and bear the arms of the Dauphin. Poitou, Aunis, Limousin, and Saintonge, were conquered by Charles V.; Guyenne and Gascony by Charles VII. Louis XI. obtained Maine and Anjou by inheritance; seized Burgundy on the death of its last Duke, Charles the Bold, in 1477; and took possession of all Provence upon the death of Charles king of Sicily, who was count of Provence. By the successive marriages of Charles and Louis with the heiress of Brittany, this last prov. was united to the crown of France. Francis I. confiscated the provs. of Auvergne, Bourbonnais, and Marche, and united them to his kingdom. By the accession of Henry IV., Bearn, Foix, and part of Gascony, were added to F. Under Henry, also, F. made its first acquisitions out of Europe, by taking possession, in 1598-1608, of large tracts of North America, Acadia, and the two Canadas,-a territory of more than 208,000 sq. m., but with a very scanty pop. In Europe, the extent of French territory had now increased to 126,416 sq. m. Under Louis the duchy of Bar, and the territories of Perpignan and Casale, comprising about 1,120 sq. m., were gained in 1633; and, nine years later, the government of Sedan, with a surface of 288 sq. m., was surrendered to the crown of F. The first possessions in the W. Indies laid claim to by France, in 1627 and 1633, were St. Kitt's, Martinique, and Guadaloupe, comprising 784 sq. m. On the continent of S. America, a colony was founded in Cayenne, by Bretigny, in 1635, which was gradually increased by conquest to 6,880 sq. m., and extended as far as Guayana. In 1642 the French government obtained possession of the island of Bourbon (1,792 sq. m.), near the SW coast of Africa. Louis left in Europe a territory of 127,856 sq. m., and in the colonies—at that time certainly of little importance—more than 217,600 sq. m., as a foundation for the splendid government of his son. Roussillon, formerly belonging to Spain, was ceded to France in 1659. Artois, formerly belonging to the Spanish Netherlands, was ceded to Louis by Charles II. of Spain. Alsace was seized by Louis and ceded to him by the peace of Ryswick in 1697. Franche-Comté was likewise seized by the same monarch in 1668, and again in 1674; and was ceded and confirmed to him by the treaties of Nimeguen and Ryswick. That part of the Netherlands which F. is still permitted to retain, was acquired by conquest during the reign of Louis XIV. The French possessions beyond Europe were also during this reign much increased in extent. Colonies were founded at Mariegalante in 1645; at Berthelemy in 1648; at Grenada in 1650; and at St. Martin in 1678. A total surface of 320 sq. m. was gained in this way. But the assumption of the whole of the W half of Hispaniola, or St. Domingo, (8,384 sq. m.), as a colony, in 1665, was of greater importance. The establishment of a factory at Chandernagore, and the acquisition of Pondicherry (192 sq. m.), laid the foundation of the French possessions in the E. Indies. At a more early period (1664) some settlements were made on the W coast of Africa, near the Senegal river, and the neighbouring island of Goree, taken in 1687 from the Dutch, making altogether a gain of 416 sq. m. On the E coast of Africa, a French expedition had erected Fort- Dauphin, in the island of Madagascar, in 1665; and the first settlement was made in the Isle of France, now called the Mauritius, in 1712. In N. America the French declared themselves, in 1648-67, the proprietors of immense tracts near the Lake Michigan, and the lands bordering thereon to the NW-a thinly peopled territory of 203,200 sq. m.; they afterwards advanced like claim to the country of the Indians at the mouth of the Mississippi, as well as Louisiana, making together 880,000 sq. m. But the most valuable acquisition in America—obtained by the peace of Utrecht, in 1713—was the island of Cape Breton, afterwards called Isle Royale, distinguished at that time for its profitable trade in furs, and productive fisheries. It nearly made up for the loss of Acadia, which, though twenty times as large, was not then of any financial value. By such steps it resulted that Louis XV. (1715-74), the great-grandson of his immediate predecessor, found himself sovereign of a large and important empire, whose territory in the heart of Europe amounted to 151,648 sq. m., and in other parts of the globe to about 1,264,000 sq. m. Notwithstanding the almost uninterrupted wars which had been waged during upwards of 200 years, F. was inferior to none of the neighbouring powers in available resources; and had even at that time a pop. in Europe of nearly 23,000,000 souls. During the reign of Louis XV. little extension of territory took place, except by pacific means, and by purchase or exchange, as in the taking possession of the island of Corsica (2,848 sq. m.), and part of the frontiers of the duchy of Savoy (208 sq. m.). Among the foreign possessions still remaining to the French crown were the colonies in the E. Indies, small in extent, but of # considerable value. In 1722 it obtained the important trading place, Mahé (8 sq. m.) on the Malabar coast; and in 1739, from the rajah of Tanjore, Caricalla, with a territory of 312 sq. m. In 1769 the French government abolished the privileges of the E. India trading company, and took possession of its property in land and colonial produce, amounting in value to about £5,367,450, engaging, in return, to become responsible for a debt of £3,461,250. In the Seven years' war, by land and sea, F. lost the major part of its immense territories in N. America. By the first treaty of Versailles in 1763, she ceded to England both the Canadas; the territory of Lake Michigan, and the country to the NW of it; likewise the island of Cape Breton, and the fisheries on the coast of Newfoundland; in the W. Indies, Grenada, with the Granadines, reserving the island of St. Lucia, taken in 1756; and lastly, she lost in Africa the colonies on the Senegal and island of Goree. In consequence of this treaty, after the lapse of six years, she also gave up to Spain, in 1769, Louisiana and New Orleans, which had never been of great value to F. Thus, at the death of Louis XV., the foreign possessions of F. were dwindled to a 25th part (29,856 sq. m.) of their former magnitude; but the French territory in Europe had increased to 159,952 sq. m., with a pop. of 25,000,000 souls, according to an official account. (4). Recent territorial arrangements.] Under the unfortunate Louis XVI., who was deprived of his crown on the 21st of September, 1792, and guillotined on the 21st of January, 1793, the French territory experienced no change from the status quo of 1774; but the success of the united French and American forces in the N. American war of independence led to a restoration of some of her considerable colonies at the peace of Versailles, in 1783. The restored possessions were those on the river Senegal, the island of Goree, the Newfoundland fisheries, with the islands of St. Pierre and Miguelon. The little island of Tobago was also regained; but, on the other hand, St. Barthelemy was sold to Sweden. Some possessions of the African company in the predatory states of N. Africa, considered as transitory acquisitions, need not be here taken into account. The total extent of the colonies of F. at the breaking out of the French revolution, was thus again increased to 30,784 sq. m. The acquisitions of the French republic during its twelve years' existence (September 21, 1792, to May 18, 1804), and of the French empire during its ten years' restless career of conquests, belong exclusively to the history of that period, inasmuch as the kingdom of F., at the restoration of the Bourbons, by the first treaty of Paris, on the 30th of May, 1814, received its ancient boundaries of the 1st of January, 1792, with regard to its European territory; and only gained the once Papal territory of Avignon, with some enclaves in what were formerly the circles of the German empire, beyond the status quo at the beginning of the reign of Louis XVI. Of the foreign possessions, as compared with those of 1792, a considerable loss was experienced by St. Domingo having, in the mean time, erected itself into the independent kingdom of Hayti. The islands of Tobago and St. Lucia, besides those of the Seychelles and Mauritius, were likewise given up to England. By the second peace of Paris, on the 20th of November, 1815, F. ceded to Prussia the territories of Saarlouis and Saarbruck; to the Netherlands, Marienburg, Philippeville and its dependencies; to Bavaria, Landau and its territory, as a protection to the German confederation; and, finally, to Sardinia, French Savoy, as well as the protectorate of the petty principality of Monaco. The extent of the French territory in Europe, according to a careful measurement and strict demarcation of its boundaries, was now fixed at 161,376 sq. m., and the possessions out of Europe at 10,512 sq. m. Divisions.] Before her first revolution, F. was divided into 32 provinces, or distinct governments. By a new arrangement in 1790, it was divided into 83 departments; and the subsequent addition of Corsica, and of the Venaissin or dep. of Vaucluse, together with the formation of the dep. of Garonneet-Tarn, completed the present number of 86 departments. But as the old divisions, though no longer subservient to the political organization of the country, still continue to be referred to, and are firmly incorporated with the language, they shall be here enumerated, along with their corresponding departments, and the population of each dep. We must also observe, that each dep. is subdivided into districts called arrondissements, of which there are 368 in the kingdom. These districts are again divided into 2,669 cantons; and finally, each canton is composed of a certain number of communes, that is to say, of towns and villages, of which there are 38,990 at present. A commune is sometimes a single town, and sometimes several villages united, possessing a mayor and communal municipality. All the considerable cities are divided into several communes. The annual resources which these communes can command consist in the real property possessed by them, the rent of the localities which they are permitted to dispose of, rents on the state and private individuals, the octroi-duties and the ex- Bearn, traordinary taxes, the produce of an assessment of 5 portion of the duties on patents, donations, legacies, fines, &c. Of 37,187 communes existing in 1837, 33,000 contained less than 1,500 inhabitants; 2,529 less than 200; and 613 less than 100. Provinces. Departments. in sq. Moselle, . Lorraine, . The most level tracts are the French Netherlands, as they are called, on the N. Coasts.] The coast of F. along the English Chanare high cliffs; and to the W of the mouth of the Seine, the coast becomes again low and sandy, but is skirted for about 16 m. by the Calvados rocks. The coasts of the dep. of La-Manche are rocky, broken by inlets, and skirted by a number of rocky islands. The W coast of Bretagne, or Finistère, is lofty and precipitous, with numerous promontories, rocks, and islets along the shore. From Finistere, the abrupt rocky hills become gradually lower, till at L'Orient, and further to the SE, they terminate in low clayey and muddy flats, in which the sea forms various inlets, chiefly at the mouths of the rivers. The headlands, however, and the numerous islands off the shore, are still of the same species of hard rock, and in some parts rise into rugged precipices. To the S of the Loire the coast becomes less broken, but low, and lined with dunes and salt marshes; and it maintains this character to the mouth of the Gironde. From the Gironde S to the Pyrenees the coast forms almost a straight line, broken only by one small inlet, and bordered by the landes, which are vast downs, links, or plains of sand, interspersed with fens, marshes, and heaths, and, at distant intervals, with meadows and cultivated fields. The coast of the gulf of Lyons from Port-Vendre to Cape Couronne, is characterized by a number of lagunes, which are separated from the sea by narrow strips of land. Near Toulon the coast assumes a bolder character; and along the gulf of Genoa it | becomes high and broken.—Along the Mediterranean, F. has 360 m. of coast-line; along the Atlantic, 585 m.; and along the North sea and channel, 525 m. Gulfs, bays, and straits.] Along the N coast of F. we have the Pas-de-Calais, or strait of Dover, between Kent in England, and Picardy in France, 21 m. wide at the narrowest part, and about 20 fath. deep in the middle; next St. John's road, to the S of Cape Grisnez; the large estuary of the Seine, full of sand-banks, with narrow intervening channels; the Grand Vay, a deep inlet between Calvados and La-Manche; the road of La-Hogue, a deep bight on the E coast of La-Manche; La Grande Ance, or Ance-de-Vauville; and Mont St. Michael's bay, on the W side of La-Manche; the Rade-de-la- Fresnay, and bay of St. Brieuc, on the coast of Cotesdu-Nord. On the W coast, there are the Passagedu-Force, the Passage-de-l'Iroise, Passage-du-Raz, road of Bertheaume, harbour of Brest, road of Camaret, and Douarnenez bay, all in Finistère; Port-Louis, the Morbihan, or gulf of Vannes, a landlocked bay, studded with islands; Quiberon bay, and the estuary of the Vilaine, in Morbihan; the estuary of the Loire, and the bay of Bourgneuf, in Loire- Inférieure and Vendée; the Pertuis-Breton, Pertuisd'Antioche, and Rade-des-Basques, between the mainland of Lower Charente and the islands of Ré and Oleron; the Gironde, the estuary of the rivers Garonne and Dordogne, obstructed at the mouth by sand-banks, through which there are five navigable channels; and the Basin-d'Arcachon, a landlocked inlet, with an entrance obstructed by sand-banks, through which there are only two narrow channels; but the principal hydrographical features are in the remaining coast-line of the gulf of Gascony. On the S or Mediterranean coast are the large gulf of Lyon, containing the minor bays of Narbonne, Martigues, Marseille, La Ciotat, and Toulon; the bay or roads of Hieres; the gulf of Grimand; the gulf of Frejos; the gulf-de-la-Napoule; the road of Gourjan; and the gulf of Nice. Capes.] The principal capes are Blanc Nez in Pas de Calais; Cape La Heve, near Havre, at the mouth of the Seine; Cape Galteville, or Barfleur, the NE, and Cape La Hague, the NW points of the peninsula of Cotentin or La Manche; and Cape Levi, on the N coast of the same department; Flamanville cape or Grosnez, and Cape Cartaret, on the W side of La Manche; Cape de Frehel in Cotes-du-Nord; St. Matthew's point, Le-Bec-de-la-Chevre, Bec-du- Raz, and Penmark point, in Finistère; the singular peninsula of Quiberon, terminating in Point de Couquet, in Morbihan; and Cap-de-la-Couronne, Cap-Croisette, Cap-de-L'Aigle, Cap-Sicie, Cap-d'Escampamberion, Cap-Benat, Cap-Taillat, Cap-St.-Tropez, Cap-de-Frejus, Cap-de-la-Garousse, all on the S or Mediterranean coast. Mountains. The mountainous districts of F. are chiefly in the S and E quarters. The principal mountain-chains are the Cévennes, the Vosges, the Jura, the Alps, and the Pyrenees.—The grand chain of the Cévennes rises to the W of the Rhone. These mountains seem to be the principal centre of the primitive district of France, and extend into several branches. The principal branch runs in a crescent form along the river Ardeche towards Ales. The 2d branch traverses the Rhone, on the side of Tournon and Vienne, towards the plains of Dauphiné. The 3d branch forms the mountains of Beaujolais, passing by Autun towards Avalon. This branch extends 70 leagues in length, but is in some parts not more than I league in breadth. The 4th branch forms the mountains of Forez, separating the basin of the Loire from that of the Allier. It runs between Roanne and Thiers towards St. Pierre le Moutier. The 5th branch separates the basin of the Allier from that of the Cher, passing by Clermont to Montlucon. The 6th branch extends in the direction of Limoges. The 7th chain stretches from the Dordogne towards the Charente; and the 8th divides the Dordogne from the Garonne. The main ridge of the Cévennes, of which the above-mentioned branches are lateral chains, runs from N to S, while the branches run in an E and W direction. The N part of the chain, or the Puy-de-Dome, is supposed to contain the volcanoes of most recent activity; the S is called Cantal; and both are denominated the mountains of Auvergne. The Monts-d'Or form the centre, and are the highest mountains in France. See CE'VENNES. -On the E borders of F., the low and rounded chain of the Vosges—the Mons Vogesus of Cæsar—rises a little to the N of Deuxponts and Keyserlautern, and runs thence in a S direction parallel to the course of the Rhine, and about 30 m. to the W of that river, separating the duchy of Deuxponts and the narrow stripe of Alsace from Lorraine and Franche-Comté. This chain extends as far as the neighbourhood of Besançon and the defiles of Porentrui on the S, where it is joined to the Jura. See Vosges.—The Jura, a vanguard of the Alps, forms the boundary between France and Switzerland, and terminates a little to the NW of Geneva. This chain, like that of the Vosges, is not very elevated. See Jura.—A chain of the Alps crosses the deps. of the Maritime Alps, Lower Alps, and Upper Alps; and afterwards stretching to the N, separates France from Italy and Switzerland, as far as the neighbourhood of the Jura. The highest summits of this chain are in the N district of the dep. of the Upper Alps. In the dep. of Drôme another Alpine ridge takes its rise, and crosses the deps. of the Ardèche, Loire, Rhone, Saone-et- Loire, and Côte-d'Or, as far as Dijon.—The Pyrenees, in the S of F., stretch from Cape Creuz, near the gulf of Roses, on the Mediterranean, to the bay of Figueras, on the coast of Spain. This vast chain, known to geographers since the days of Herodotus, may be considered as equally belonging to France and Spain. Granite is the basis of this chain; but the Pyrenees have been found to present calcareous appearances; and even sea-shells are found upon their highest summits, which are about the middle of the chain, and belong to F. To the S and E the Pyrenees present nothing but dreadful sterility; but on the N and E the descent is more gradual, and affords frequent woods and pastures. As the level country on the French side of the Pyrenees is much lower than on the Spanish side, the Pyrenees appear much more lofty when viewed from the plains of the Garonne than when seen from the high land of Spain; but it is the opinion of the most celebrated naturalists, that the inclination of the S side of the chain is considerably more precipitous than that of F., and access from this side is generally more laborious, for the French valleys ascend to the crest of the chain by an easy and gradual rise, an arrangement which is by no means so marked on the Spanish side. The principal defiles leading from F. into Spain, are those from St.-Jean-de-Luz to Irun, from St.-Jean-Pied-de-Port to Roncesvalles and Pampeluna, and from Perpignan to Barcelona. See Pyrenees. Rivers.] F. is everywhere intersected with rivers and streams. According to native geographers there are 6,000 rivers in F., of which 300 are navigable. Omitting streams whose length of course is under 40 m., 5 rivers, whose courses are either wholly or partly within F., flow into the North sea or German ocean, culture, and richness of its banks, all conspire to render its upper course eminently beautiful; but in the lower part of its course its character is changed and all its beauty lost.—The Garonne rises in Spain, and at Bourdeaux it receives the Dordogne. The united stream, called the Gironde, enters the sea by two channels near the town of Cordovan, after a course of 250 m. The scenery of the Garonne between Toulouse and Bourdeaux is beautiful, the river rolling its course through extensive plains whose luxuriant fertility cannot be exceeded; but the mouth of the Gironde is girt with rocks and barren deserts.—The Seine near Paris carries heavier vessels than any other river in Europe, in proportion to the length of its course and breadth of its channel. Its whole course is about 490 m. The valley of the Seine above Rouen is not surpassed by any of the rivervalleys in Europe, in point of fertility, beauty, and expansion.—The reader is referred to our special articles on the different rivers of F. for fuller details respecting the internal hydrography of F. Ib., De Remoray, De Chaillexon, Hautes-Pyrenees, Doredon, D'Aydat, There are a considerable number of etangs, or lakes which have no visible outlet, in the deps. of Ain, Cher, Indre, Meurthe, and Saone-et-Loire. Climate.] The climate of a country so extensive as F. must necessarily be various; yet it may be regarded, on the whole, as perhaps more favourable to the sustenance and comfort of human life than that of any other region in Europe. In the N districts the climate is hotter and more moist in summer than in the SW parts of England. The humidity of the climate of Normandy is fully proved by the beautiful verdure of its rich pastures; yet, even at a distance from the coast, the rains in the N of France are extremely heavy, and of longer duration than in England. In winter they experience heavier snows, and more severe frosts, than occur in the S of England.—The central division of F. possesses the best climate. In Touraine and the Limousin, snow and frost seldom occur; the air is pure, light and elastic, and the spring a continuance of such weather as is enjoyed in England about the middle of May. The harvest commences about the latter end of June, but is sometimes so late as the middle of July. The great heats are from the middle of July to the middle of August. Still, however, the climate of the central provinces has its disadvantages. All the violent storms of hail and rain; the former occasiongreatest heats seldom occur till the 15th of July, nor as the pleasantest months in the year. In December, January, and February, the weather is generally fine; but, after February, the wind called the bise or mistral is very frequent. Vegetable zones.] The climate of F. naturally divides itself into four zones, according to the vegetable produce which each affords. The most northern zone considerably resembles England in vegetation and climate; the second differs from the preceding chiefly in exhibiting here and there a few vineyards; fields of maize begin to make their appearance in the third; and the fourth is distinguished from all the former by the intermixture of olives and mulberries with corn, vines, and maize.—Coucy 10 m. N of Soissons, Clermont in the Beauvaisais, Beaumont in Maine, and Herbignac in Bretagne, mark the dividing line between vines and no vines. The separating line between maize and no maize is first seen on the W side of the kingdom, in going from Angoumois and entering Poitou at Verac, near Ruffec; and is met with in crossing Lorraine, between Nancy and Luneville. These lines are not parallel to the degrees of latitude; but proceed in an oblique line from SW to NE, parallel to each other. The line which is formed by the vines is nearly unbroken; but that formed by the maize, in the central part of France, proceeds no farther N than the S of the Limousin. The line of olives is also pretty nearly in the same oblique direction from NE to SW. It tier, and Montelimart, upon the Rhone, S of Lyons. Hence, Young concludes that the E parts of France indicate by their productions 21 degrees latitude of more heat than the W,—a generalization somewhat erroneous. The surface of F. rises gradually towards the E, and has consequently a lower mean temperature on the E side than on the W; and the heat is more unequally distributed in the seasons, the winter being more vigorous, the summer more ardent: Hence the E provinces are better fitted for the culture of such plants as being annual like maize, or losing their leaves like the vine, totally escape the severity of winter. The W side of France, on the other hand, is better suited to the growth of such plants as shrink from cold, such as the kermes-oak, the cork-tree, and the fig-tree. Temperature.] As to temp., the annual heat of London and Paris is nearly the same; but, from the beginning of April to the end of October, the heat is greater at Paris than London. If the annual temp. of London,—the average degree of cold in January, —and the average degree of heat in July,—be represented by 1.000; the annual temp. of Paris may be represented by 1.028; the average degree of cold in January at 1.040; and of heat in July by 1.037. The annual temp. of Bordeaux will be represented by 1.090; the average degree of cold in January, by 925; and the average degree of heat in July by 1.139. The annual temp. of Montpellier will be represented by 1.170; the average degree of cold in January by ·850; and the average degree of heat in July by 1.196. State of the atmosphere.] As to the state of the atmosphere, in the neighbourhood of Paris the barometer never continues 24 hours without changing. In the W districts it rises and falls sooner than in the E. From a series of 15,000 barometrical observations made in order to calculate the influence of the winds on the barometer, Mr. Burckhard found that the S wind gave, for a mean height, 27 inches 11 lines 3 cent., while an E wind raised the mercury to 28 inches 1 line 9 cent. He also found that the height of the barom, on the Mediterranean shores was 28° 22 lines; while its height on the Atlantic shores was 28° 2' 8 lines. In central France the barometer's greatest height, on an average of several years, is 20 inches 5' 7 lines; the least 27° 3' 3 lines. In the N of France the greatest height is 27° 10′ 10 lines; the least 26° 5′ 8 lines. In the W the average height of the barom. is 28° 3 lines. At Montpellier, in the SE, the greatest height is 28° 5' 3 lines; the least 27° 5′ 5 lines. At Marseilles the greatest height is 28° 7′ 2 lines; the least 27° 3′ 7 lines. Winds.] According to Kæmp, the mean direction of the wind throughout all France is S 88° W. that is on the points of an azimuth of 88° counting passes through Carcassonne, near the Spanish fron- | from S to W. It appears, from a continued series * of observations made in different parts of France, | that in the centre the prevailing winds are the SW and NE. In the N, the SE wind is most common. The N and SW winds prevail in the E; in the W, the NE wind; at Montpellier, the N and NE winds. The SE and NW winds prevail at Marseilles.—The mean quantity of rain that falls annually at Paris is 22 inches. The evaporation is generally greater than the rain: the mean evaporation being 23 in. In the centre of France, the average quantity of rain is rather more than 20 in. The average number of rainy days in the year is 164. In the N of France there are 126 rainy days; in the E, 145; in the W, 150. At Montpellier there are 74 rainy days, and the quantity of rain is upwards of 27 in.,—a proof of the violence of the rain when it falls. At Marseilles the quantity of rain is rather more than 21 inches, and the number of rainy days 57. Geology.] A general idea of the geology of F. may be obtained by conceiving it to form an extensive basin, of which the circumference consists of primitive formations, and of which the centre also rises in primitive rocks; while the intermediate space between the central formation and the circumference is composed of rocks of secondary and tertiary classes. This general arrangement may be seen from the following table:- 1. Alpes. Granitic and 2. Pyrenees. 3. Vosges. Schistose, Mountainous, 4. Maures. 5. Jura. Calcareous, Provence. Granitic 7. Central plateau. and 8. Ardenne. Schistose, 9. Bretagne. Plateaus, 10. Causses. 11. Languedoc. 12. Quercy. Calcareous, 13. Ht. Poitou. 14. Bourgogne. Lorraine. Champagne. 17. Neustria. 18. Aquitaine. Plains, 19. Limagne. 20. Bresse. 21. Alsace. The several formations of F. are marked by members of every group of the stratified system, from the non-fossiliferous strata to those of the tertiary and alluvial classes; and likewise igneous rocks, from the older granite and trap to the more recent extinct volcanoes. On its S and E boundaries, on the flanks of the Pyrenees, and the Alps of Switzerland, we find gneiss and mica slate; and also in the central district of Auvergne mica slate is sometimes seen bounding that volcanic region. In Bretagne is exhibited the older greywacke slate, including large crystals of the curious mineral chiastolite. On its NE confines the Silurian system is developed, containing impressions of the trilobite. In several other districts the same system—the transition of Werner-is developed, as between the Rhine and the Moselle; on the confines of Normandy; and along the course of the Loire, from the centre of F. to its S extremity. In the NE, and in some other localities, it underlies the coal formation; and in the Vosges it is metalliferous. The coal formation group also exists in F., frequently connected with the mountain limestone; but coal in F. is unaccompanied with iron-stone, as in Britain,—a circumstance which greatly affects the manufacturing interests of that country; for although iron-stone is found, it is at a distance from the fuel necessary for smelting it. The coal deposits in F. generally rest on the greywacke formation. In some places they rest on granite. In Normandy there is a small coal-basin; but the most abundant deposits occur in the central that one prodigious mass of fine sand covers the parts, as in the valleys of the Loire, Allier, Creuse, Dordogne, Aveyron, and the Ardèche; and in the SE districts between the Rhone and the Cévennes. In the deps. bordering on Belgium the coal formation is rather extensive, commencing to the E of Boulogne, and proceeding in an easterly course for above 100 m., and is prolonged into Belgium. Although the quantity of coal worked in F. is considerable, it furnishes comparatively but a small proportion of the fuel consumed in the smelting and preparation of metals, in factories, and for domestic purposes; nor is its quality equal to that of England and Scotland. The members of the new red sandstone group extend over several tracts. In Normandy it is of no great extent, but from the Ardennes to the Vosges, including the intermediate districts of the Meuse, the Moselle, and the Meurthe, it exhibits all its characters of variegated sandstones, marls, gypsum, limestone, and rock-salt. In an E direction this formation passes into the district of the Lower Rhine. In the Vosges, the muschelkalk —a shell limestone of peculiar organic characters—is extracted and used as a marble. In this limestone alone has been found the beautiful lily encrinite. The oolitic system is so intricate in its windings that it is difficult to follow its course. It nearly surrounds the Paris basin. From Caen, in the N, it trends E through Beauvais to Luxemburg, while another branch proceeds in a S direction, and W of Paris, through Maine and Anjou to Poitiers, and then turns W to Rochelle. On the E of Paris, a third branch, commencing at Luxemburg, extends along the course of the Moselle, and the Meurthe, the Vosges, through Cote-d'Or, along the course of the Saone, forming part of the Jura chain, and onwards to the confines of Lyons. This group is also ramified through other deps. of F., and flanks the Pyrenees and the Swiss Alps. In all these localities, and in all its subdivisions, from the lias to its upper beds, the oolitic group is well developed in its mineral characters, and abounds in finely preserved organic remains. From the examination of the geological phenomena, the remarkable conclusion has been deduced regarding the age of the Alpine regions of Switzerland, and other districts on the confines of F., that the close of the oolitic period marks the era of the elevation of those places; and consequently, that F. and Switzerland existed only as the bottom of the sea, while the greater part of Scotland, and a considerable portion of the W of England, were elevated into dry land. In F., too, the greensand and chalk, or cretaceous formations, are extensively developed, covering the oolitic group, and following its sinuosities, except on the confines of the Netherlands, where the chalk overlies the coal transition slates. Everywhere this group is well characterised by its numerous and peculiar fossils, in a fine state of preservation. But the geology of F. has been brought most prominently into notice by the contents of its tertiary basin. The basin of the Seine is separated for a long space from that of the Loire by an extensive high plain, the greater part of which bears the name of Beauce. This plain is bounded towards the NW by a higher and more broken district, from which the rivers Eure, Aure, Orne, Maienne, Sarte, &c., arise. On all other sides the plain of Beauce overlooks every surrounding district. The slope from it towards the Seine is divided into two inclinations, one of which, on the W, looks towards the Eure, and the other, on the E, looks towards the Seine. These two inclined plains, however, are not straight, but in all directions unequal and rugged, the slopes are generally very abrupt, and all the ravines, valleys, and wells dug in the high parts, show whole surface, passing equally over all the other soils or inferior platforms which this great plain overlooks. The edge of this platform towards the Seine forms the natural limit of the basin of Paris on this side. From below the two extremities of this platform issue two portions of a platform of chalk, which extends in every direction to a great distance, forming the whole of Higher Normandy, Picardy, and Champagne. In some parts of the two latter provs. the chalk is covered with sandy platforms, similar to that of Beauce. Hence it appears that the materials which compose the basin of Paris have been deposited in a vast gulf, the bottom of which is chalk. This chalk lies in horizontal beds, with flints, and is wholly or in part covered by certain argillaceous, siliceous, calcareous, gypseous, and alluvial strata. The basin, measuring directly from Epernay to Gisars, nearly from E to W, is 87 m. in length, and from Nemours to the neighbourhood of Noyon, nearly S and N, it is 70 m. broad. On the SW, from near Nemours to the mouth of the rivulet called the Maulde, a direct distance of about 45 m., it is limited by a covering stratum of Beauce sand, and in all its remaining sides by the naked chalk stratum. The chalk is undoubtedly the most ancient, and the sandy platform the most recent of the formations in this basin. Between them there are two great strata; the first, lime, either siliceous containing no shells, or lime with coarse shells; the second, which is named by the French geologists gypso-marley, is not generally spread, but merely scattered in spots, very different from one another in thickness and in their component parts. These two intermediate soils or strata, as well as the two extreme strata of chalk and sand, and all the vacuities which they have left, are partly filled by a fifth sort of soil, mixed also with marl and silex, which may be called fresh water soil, because it abounds in fresh water soils only. The lowest fresh water contains much gypsum or sulphate of lime, from which plaster of Paris is prepared. In this member of the group have been disinterred numerous osseous remains of mammiferous quadrupeds, an order of creation of which no remains have ever been discovered in any older formation, if we except traces of the didelphis, observed in England in the oolite. A country whose surface is so varied as that of F., may be expected to exhibit evidences of the agencies by which the aqueous rocks have been elevated. Accordingly its confines—the Alpine regions of Switzerland and the Pyrenees—furnish abundant evidence of gigantic igneous action, where granite peaks have burst through and elevated the stratified systems, and along the flanks of which granite is often ramified. In the central provs. of Auvergne, ridges of granite —associated with gneiss—rise to the height of 3,000 ft., and form the E and W boundaries of that volcanic region. Here, too, and in the adjoining prov. of Vivarais, are extensive masses, dykes, and overlying caps of the trap series, basalts and greenstones often beautifully columnar. Besides these, numerous volcanic cones rise to a great elevation, the summit of the highest, Puy-de-Dome, being 4,846 ft. above sealevel. Of the activity of this volcanic region there is no written or traditionary record, the epoch of eruption having been probably anterior to the historical period, though certainly later than the tertiary era. The average extent of this region is from 40 to 50 m. in length, and 20 m. in breadth. The plains and surrounding country are extremely fertile, the felspathic lavas in particular being highly favourable to vegetation. Soil.] The soil of F. varies much in different parts of the king dom; but it may be remarked, that there is scarcely any kind of soil in it which in England could be called a clay soil. The soils may be classed as follows: 1st, Fat loam, of different degrees of tenacity. The N district of this soil extends over the provs. of Flanders, Artois, Picardy, Normandy, and the Isle of France On the coast, it may be said to extend from Dunkirk to Carentum in Normandy. From thence to Coutances, the land is chiefly poor and stony, and continues so, though with some variations, to Brest. In a line a little to the S of the coast before Caen, the first considerable change of soil from Calais is seen. In Normandy, on the side of Alençon, rich loams on a calcareous bottom are met with. From Dunkirk to Nemours is not less than 180 m. in a right line; from Soissons to Carentan is another right line of about 200 m; from Eu, on the coast of Normandy, to Chartres, is 100 m. The breadth of this rich district in some places, especially about Caen, &c., is not considerable, yet the whole will be found to contain not a trifling proportion of the kingdom. The fertile plains of Flanders and of Artois are perhaps the richest parts of this valuable soil, which here consists of deep friable mould, rather inclining to clay than sand, on a calcareous bottom, bearing evident marks of having once been covered by the sea. From Paris to near Cambray, by the road of Soissons, this loam is more sandy, but equally valuable and fertile. About Meaux there is as fine soil as can possibly exist. It consists of an almost impalpable powder, and of admirable texture and friability; in some places 18 ft. deep, resting on a stratum of white marl. The line from Paris through Picardy is inferior; but all the arable part of Normandy which lies within the limits above described, is a rich, friable, sandy loam, in some places of a reddish colour, and very deep.—The calcareous loams are of much greater extent than the loams which have been described. To the E, they stretch across Champagne. From Metz to Nancy all is calcareous, but not chalk. In the S parts of Alsace limestone land abounds. Immense districts of Dauphiny and Provence consist of the same kind of soil. Indeed the chalk-district extends E to about St. Menehould, and S to Nemours and Montargis, or even farther, for it reaches Auxerre in another direction. There is also much calcareous loam in Angoumois, Poitou, and through Touraine to the Loire. Most of the course of this river is calcareous. The chalk-district, therefore, may be regarded as stretching not less than 200 m. E and W, and about as much, but more irregularly, N and S.—The next considerable district of fertile soil is the plain of the Garonne. Through all this plain, wherever the soil is found excellent, it consists usually of a deep, mellow, friable, sandy loam, sufficiently moist, and in many places calcareous. The plain of the Garonne is entered about Creissensac, in passing S from the Limousin. Its fertility increases all the way to Toulouse, where it is uncommonly rich; its richness, however, diminishes as we approach the Pyrenees. The breadth of this plain is everywhere inconsiderable. Another tract of rich soil is found in the vale, which stretches from Narbonne to Beziers, Montpellier, and Nismes: but its fertility is inferior to those that have been previously described. The soil of the Lower Poitou resembles that of the richest parts of the Lincolnshire fens, and is indeed of the same nature, being for the most part marshy land drained, or gained from the sea. To the S of the Loire, in the direction of Bourgneuf, there is a tract of rich loam. Alsace, in respect to soil, resembles Flanders, but it is inferior to it. The whole fertile part of the narrow plain of Alsace, hardly presents a surface of more than 1,000 sq. m. The flat and chiefly calcareous vale of Auvergne, which commences at Riom, is a tract of great fertility. The whole surface is a real marl, but mixed with such a proportion of soil as to be most valuable and productive. The French naturalists who have examined it, assert the depth to be 20 ft. of beds of earth formed of the ruins of primitive and volcanized mountains. The best part of this vale reaches no farther than from Riom to Vaires, scarcely more than 20 m. Mr. Young calculates, that the whole of the fertile districts of F., which we have just described, amounts to about 28,000,000 of English acres. 2d, The district of heath is chiefly in the provs. of Brittany. Anjou, parts of Normandy, and Guienne, and Gascony. The 5 departments into which Brittany is divided, are reckoned to contain 1,609 French sq. m. The cultivated land amounts, according to some calculations, to less than one-third, and the heaths to 3,006,000 acres. According to other calculations, two-fifths of the whole prov. are uncultivated; and some authors assert, that of 39 parts, 24 are landes. Some of the heaths are so extensive that a house is scarcely seen in 10 leagues. The soil of the best part of the heaths in Brittany is commonly gravel, or gravelly sand on a gravelly bottom, of a very inferior and barren nature. In many places it rests on sandstone rock: none of it is calcareous. Anjou and Maine are equally noted for the immensity of their heaths, which are reported to extend 60 leagues in one place. The soil of these heaths is, however, in some parts tolerably good, and might be rendered useful by proper skill and labour; consisting of gravel, sand, or stone, generally a loamy sand or gravel. —The Landes, as they are emphatically called, lie W from Bazadois and Condomois to the sea-coast, between the country of Labour on the S, Guienne on the N, and the ocean on the W. They are divided into the greater Landes between Bourdeaux and Bayonne, and the lesser between Bazas and Montmarsan. They are sandy tracts, covered with pine trees, cut regularly for resin, broken and enlivened however with cultivated spots for a league or two. When the Moors were expelled from Spain, they applied to the court of F. to be allowed to settle on and cultivate these lands; but permission was not granted them. They are said to contain not less than 300 sq. leagues, or 1,468,181 acres, occupying a large portion of Gascony. Though their soil is among the poorest in France, it is not utterly incapable of cultivation, and even the pines with which it is covered yield from 15s. to 20s. an acre. 3d, The district of chalk, as distinguished from the calcareous loams already noticed, is chiefly in the provs. of Champagne, Sologne, Touraine, Poitou, Saintonge, and Angoumois. The chalk-provinces contain 16,000,000 of acres. The soil of Champagne in general is thin and poor. The S part, as from Chalons to Troyes, has from its poverty acquired the name of poulleux, or 'lousy.' Sologne is one of the poorest and most unimproved provs. in the kingdom. It is a flat, consisting of a poor sand, or gravel lying everywhere on a bottom of clay or marl, so very retentive of water that every ditch and hole is full of it, except in the driest seasons. Touraine is better; and contains some considerable districts, especially on the S of the Loire, in which good mixed sandy and gravelly loams rest on a calcareous bottom. Considerable tracts in the N parts of the prov., however, are of a very inferior soil, not better than the predominating soil of Anjou and Maine. Poitou consists of two divisions, the upper and the lower, the latter of which has already been mentioned as resembling the fens of Lincolnshire. The upper division is generally a thin loam, on an imperfect quarry bottom,-a very inferior stone brash. A great part of Angoumois is a thin and poor chalk. 4th, The district of gravel is chiefly in the Bourbonnois and Nivernois. The gravel in the latter is of little value, three-fourths of the prov. being covered with heath, broom, or wood; but notwithstanding the inferiority of the soil, these provs, were reckoned by Mr. Young among the most improveable in France. They form one vast plain, through which the Loire and Allier pass. The better parts of them consist of a sandy soil, and in some places the sub-soil is calcareous. In a few spots good friable sandy loams are met with. 5th, The district of stony soils is chiefly in Lorraine, Burgundy, Franche-Compté, &c. In Lorraine there are commons of immense extent which scarcely yield anything. From St. Menehould to the borders of Alsace the soil is entirely stony, but of various kinds; a great part is of the kind denominated 'stonebrash' in England, "or the broken triturated surface of imperfect quarries, mixed by time, frost, and cultivation, with some loam and vegetable mould;" much is calcareous. Districts of rich and even deep friable loams occur in Lorraine, but they are of inconsiderable extent. The soil of Burgundy varies much: the best part of it lies in the line from Franche-Compte to the Bourbonnois by Dijon. Here sandy and gravelly loams predominate; but even in this part of it there are spots of poor granite soil. The subdivision of the prov. called Bresse is a poor country; the grounds alone, on a white clay or marl, amounting it is said to nearly 250,000 acres. The stony soil of Franche-Compté is in general good. From Besançon to Orechamp the country is rocky quite to the surface. The rocks are calcareous. A reddish brown loam rests on the rock. In the hilly parts a red ferruginous loam, schistus, and gravel predominate. Part of Alsace consists of soil of nearly the same character. 6th, The district of various loams, mixed with sand, granite, gravel, stone, &c., is chiefly in the Limousin, La Manche, Berry, &c. The loams of the two former are friable and sandy; some on granite, and others on a calcareous sub-soil. Of the granite there are two kinds; one hard, and full of micaceous particles, the grain coarse, with but little quartz, hardening in the air in masses, but becoming a powder when reduced to small pieces. This is very unfertile, as neither wheat, vines, nor chestnuts will grow on it. The other sort is in horizontal strata mixed with great quantities of spar. On it these plants thrive well. This kind of granite and chestnuts appear together on entering Limousin; but on the road to Toulouse, where there is about a league of hard granite, this tree disappears. Berry has a poor soil, though not so poor as that of Sologne. In some places it is sandy or gravelly; in other places the loams are more tenacious, lying on quarries of stone or lime. 7th, The provs. of Auvergne, Dauphiny, Provence, the Lyonnois, Languedoc, and Rousillon, contain the mountainous district. The mountains that surround the vale of the Limagne of Auvergne are various. The white argillaceous stone in the hills, between Riom and Clermont, is calcareous. The volcanic mountains are more fertile than the others, except where they are composed of tufa, or cinders, which are so burnt as to be good for nothing. The calcareous and clayey mountains are good; and the basaltic, when decomposed, form excellent clay. The base is commonly granite. Many considerable mountains, in the tract from Le Puy to Montelimart, are also volcanic, and extremely fertile.-Provence and Dauphiny, with the exception of a few plains and valleys, are mountainous. Of these, the former is the driest with respect to soil in the kingdom. Rock and sandy gravels abound; and the course of the Durance is so ruined by sand and shingle, that, on a moderate calculation, above 130,000 acres have been thus destroyed. If Dauphiny were divided into three parts, three-fourths of one part, it is calculated, would be cultivated land; more than three-fourths of another part would be mountainous and uncultivated; and half the third part mountainous and in culture. The mountainous districts of Dauphiny and Provence are generally calcareous. The whole coast of Provence is a poor stony soil, with very few exceptions. The Lyonnois is mountainous in many parts, the soil being poor, stony, and rough, with much waste land. Seven-eighths of the prov. of Languedoc are mountainous. The vale lands are rich. Rousillon is in general calcareous. Much of it flat and very stony, as well as dry and barren. Mr. Young observes, "that the proportion of poor land in England to the total of the kingdom, is greater than the similar proportion in F.; nor have they anywhere such tracts of wretched blowing sand as are to be met with in Norfolk and Suffolk Thin heaths, moors, and wastes, not mountainous, which the French term landes, and which are so frequent in Brittany, Anjou, Maine, Guienne, and Gascony, are inmittely better than our northern moors; and the mountains of Scotland and Wales cannot be compared, in point of soil, with those of the Pyrenees, Auvergne, Dauphiny, Provence, and Languedoc." According to the same author, the following are the proportional areas of the several divisions of the kingdom, classed according to their respective soils, and determined by a process of approximative admeasurement to be afterwards noticed: Acres Acres. Rich district of the NE, containing the provs, of Flanders, Artois, Picardy, Normandy, the Isle-of-France, &c., . aine, Poitou, Saintonge, Angoumois, &c., . . The district of gravel of the Bourbonnois and Niver- Total, . It is to be remarked, however, that this admeasurement includes the whole surface of the kingdom: deductions ought therefore to be made for roads and rivers, &c. Mines and Minerals. Only one out of the 86 departments into which modern F. is divided, adds nothing to the mineral wealth of the country. The principal products of the other deps. are coal-including the varieties called lignite and anthraciteiron and steel, silver and lead, antimony, copper, manganese, alum, and sulphate of iron. The total value of the quantities of these substances produced in 1832, was £4,230,039; in 1836, £6,169,138. This great increase, amounting to nearly 45 per cent. within four years, was in no small degree to be attributed to the system of inspection established by the government in 1833, Since that period the French mining proprietors have had the advantage, at stated intervals, of the advice of inspecting engineers regarding the conduct of their works. The government has established a board-of-commissioners, under the control of the minister of the interior, under the title of 'Direction Générale des Ponts et Chaussées et des Mines.' This board has under its orders a competent staff of well-educated engineers, part of whose duty it is to collect the statistical details of the works they are appointed to inspect. Iron.] From a report in which these details are embodied, and in which the amount and value of the mineral industry of each dep. of F. during the year 1834, are given with a high degree of minuteness and of accuracy, it appears that more than two-thirds of the value created in F. by mining industry belongs to its ironworks. The quantity of ore extracted from the whole of the iron-mines in 1834 amounted to 1,551,473 tons, of which the value was £144,252, which was considerably increased by the cost of labour in dressing the ore, and of carriage to the furnaces. The number of smelting furnaces in use was 374; and the weight of iron produced 221,886 tons: in 1814 it was only 100,000 tons. About five-sixths of this quantity was made in the form of pigiron, and the remainder into castings of various kinds. The value added to the material by these operations was £1,297,502. There were in use in 1834, 97 furnaces for converting the ore at once into malleable iron and steel, by a peculiar process. The quantities of each metal produced in this manner were,-iron, 8,531 tons; steel, 399 tons. The total value of the French iron manufacture amounted to £3,492,519. Five-sixths in value of the fuel used in the iron manufacture was the produce of the forests of F. The other kinds of fuel,-viz., bituminous coal and turf, or peat, made up the remaining sixth; the total value of the fuel used being £1,449,338. The total number of workmen employed was 31,704, exclusive of those employed in transporting the ore, in procuring fuel, carbonizing wood and coal, and conveying them to the smelting-works and forges. The value of the iron and steel produced in 1836, amounted to £4,975,424, and the following abstract presents, under five principal divisions, the total number of workmen engaged in the manufacture, with the value created by them in each of those divisions:- Number Value of workmen. created. Total, In 1845, according to an article in the Moniteur on the produce and manufacture of iron in F., the number engaged in the mines and works were estimated at 51,000; and the other individuals employed in carrying the metal, and in various external works, being as numerous, the iron industry, it was calculated, afforded employment to at least 100,000 men. The produce in 1845 exceeded in value 166,000,000 francs. To that human force must be added 2,047 hydraulic engines and 207 steam engines, representing together a power equal to that of 26,504 horses. The extraction from 425 mines open in 1845, produced a total the 432 forges in operation out of 608 into 439,000 tons of cast iron and 342,000 tons of bar iron. To show the great progress made by the iron industry in F., the Moniteur gives the following Iron. Cast iron. Thus, in 20 years, the produce of iron in F. had more than doubled. It was calculated that the smelting and working of iron consumed the whole iron foundries in F. produced 425,000 tons of cast metal, of the value of 59,000,000 francs; and 275,000 tons of pigiron, of the value of $1,000,000 francs. Coal.] It is commonly supposed that F. is ill-supplied with coal, and that this is one great cause of her inferiority to England in manufacturing industry: the prevailing opinion, however, involves at least one error, and possibly two. It is true that the seat of the principal manufactures in England and Scotland is generally in juxtaposition with the great coal-fields; but this does not hold universally, for in Scotland we have coal where there are no manufactures, -witness Mid-Lothian; and manufactures where there is no coal,—witness Aberdeen, Perth and Dundee. Norwich presents an example of the latter kind in England. In thickly peopled countries, some manufactures, such as those of iron, can only be carried on advantageously where coal is abundant; there are others, such as those of silk and hosiery, in conducting which, cheap fuel is probably of less importance than cheap food, and where of course a plentiful corn-country, and great facility of communication, are the prime advantages. But whatever causes may have given birth to the manufactures of England, or determined their location, it is certain that the inferiority of F. in this species of industry, is not the consequence of a deficient supply of coal. Little coal is used in that country for domestic purposes, because wood is cheap, and is preferred by the people; and this, we have no doubt, is one reason why the former species of fuel has been supposed to be scarce; but the fact is, that France is rich in coal. Every one who has seen London from the top of St. Paul's or of the Monument, and Paris from the towers of Notre Dame or the heights of Montmartre, must have been struck by the peculiar aspect which the absence of smoke gives the latter city. The buildings stand out in bold and prominent relief, without anything to soften their stiff outlines; and, on the other hand, without any fog or mist to obscure the definition of their form. It is, of course, an article of faith with all Parisians that this absence of smoke contributes equally to the beauty of the city and the health of its inhabitants. So early as 1520, complaints were made of the introduction of English coals for domestic purposes into Paris, and the subject was referred to the consideration of the faculty of medicine. A committee of inquiry was appointed; and, after some delay, an elaborate report was presented, stating that, with certain precautions, mineral coal might be consumed without involving any injury to public health.
