When prejudices were removed the new combustion began to be regarded with favour. Henry IV., to encourage mining operations, remitted the tithe on coal, to which minerals were subjected, as being what the feudal laws called 'royalties.' Louis XIV., one of the greatest patrons of the system of protection, imposed a duty on foreign coals to encourage home production. This, at least, is the reason assigned in the royal edict; but many writers have ascribed the tax to a more selfish motive—the monarch's dread that the consumption of coal would interfere with the consumption of firewood from the royal forests, which formed an important item of the public revenue. In 1834 four-fifths of the entire production of coal in F. were drawn from the four deps. of Loire, Nord, Saone-et-Loire, and Aveyron. The * number of coal-mines was stated to be 209, of which only 140 were worked during that year. The quantity of coal extracted was 1,550,530 tons, the value of which, at 7s. 6d. per ton, amounted to £581,448. In 1842, there were 258 coal-mines in process of being worked, and 681 pits. The quantity of coal extracted was 35,920,843 metrical quintals. The consumption, including the imports, amounted to 52,034,156 metrical quintals. The value of the coal extracted from French mines in 1842 was estimated at 33,497,779 francs; the average price was 93 centimes per metrical quintal. In that year 28,788 workmen were employed in these mines-22,036 interiorly and 6,752 exteriorly. duction was 4,202,000 tons; the imports, 2,141,000 tons. From 1844 to 1845 the number of coal-pits increased from 425 to 449; that of the steam-engines employed in extraction from 301 to 391; and the production from 3,782,000 tons to 4,202,000 tons, of which 1,000,000 was furnished by the basin of the Loire; 946,000 by that of Valenciennes, 300,000 by the Creuzot and Blanzy, 65,000 by Aubin, 106,000 by Commentry, &c. In 1847, 77 coalbasins were known to exist in 56 different deps. in F. In 14 of these deps. there was no working of the mines whatever, in 17 others they did not draw above 20,000 cwt.; in 13 others but 5,000 tons were drawn from the mines in the year. There were in fact but 25 mines of any consequence. The 16 richest of these gave the following result: Basin of the Loire, Alais. *** Creuzotaud-Blanzy, La-Capelle, . *** ... Bert, ... St. Pierre, 153,654 307,994 ... ... Lignite is found in 14 deps., chiefly in those bordering on the Mediterranean. About three-fifths of the entire produce is procured in the dep. of the Bouches-du-Rhone. The number of mines of this mineral was 75, but only 48 were in operation in 1834. Their produce amounted in quantity to 69,177 tons, and in value to £22,314.—Anthracite is produced in the four deps. of Isere, Mayenne, Sarthe, and Hautes-Alpes. These mines have been worked only a few years; their produce has been quadrupled since 1828, and is still rapidly increasing. Out of 32 mines, 24 yielded in 1834, 38,398 tons, the value of which was £20,483, and the number of workmen employed was 533. Of the produce of 1835, 101,508 tons (value £39,433) consisted of lignite, and 57,603 tons (value £32,532) were composed of anthracite, leaving 1,957,022 tons (value £793,281) as the quantity of common coal raised in that year.—Mineral bitumen is found in the three deps. of L'Ain, Puy de Dome, and Bas-Rhin. This branch of industry is at present greatly on the increase. The quantity of purified bituminous product amounted in 1834 to 870 tons, and its value to £7,025.—Peat is found in greater or less quantity in more than 40 of the departments of France. In some few of these it has been procured for many centuries, but in the greater part, through ignorance or prejudice, the inhabitants have not availed themselves of its advantages until the present century. The bogs in F. amount to 2,527, of which 460 were not worked. They occupied in 1842, 59,000 persons, and produced 6,565,814 metrical quintals, estimated at 5,326,184 francs, or 81 centimes per metrical quintal. Turf is principally used in sugar-bakeries, distilleries, dye-houses, and lime-kilns. Lead, silver, &c.] The lead-mines of F., 29 in number, are situated in 18 deps.; but only 10 mines were productive in 1834. Their produce that year was, Silver, 4,442 pounds Troy weight, value £18,572 Lead, 499 tons value 7,268 - - value Litharge, &c., 317 tons 5,075 The conversion of pig-lead into sheets, pipes, &c., added about £40,000 to the value of the metal. The whole value of the lead produced in 1836 by the eleven lead-mines of F., amounted to £16,209, of which £11,692 came from the mine of Finistere. Only one of the other ten mines produced lead to the value of £1,000 in the same year.—The silver produce of the year 1836 amounted in value to £16,650, and of this sum £11,542 worth was yielded by the lead-mine of Finistere. The remaining £5,108 of silver was separated from the produce of other three of the lead-mines. The importations of lead into France from foreign countries, principally Spain, averaged 14,800 tons per annum, for the five years preceding 1837.—The copper-mines in F. are nine in number; but only two were in operation, and the produce of these was inconsiderable, and rapidly decreasing. In the year 1834, the total produce amounted only to 135 tons. There are numerous works in F. on a large scale for the manufacture of various articles of copper, brass, and bronze, but the metallic copper used is almost wholly of foreign production, and chiefly that of Russia and England.— Gold mines anciently existed in the S of F.; and some of the rivulets still roll down particles of this metal. The only mine of gold that has been discovered, in modern times, is at La-Gardette, in the valley of Oysans, in the dep. of Isere. This event took place in 1791. It is a regular vein of quartz, traversing a mountain of gneiss, and containing auriferous sulphuret of iron, and some fine specimens of native gold; but the produce did not pay the expense, and this mine has been abandoned.—Antimony is found in the mountains of Auvergne and the Vivarais; but the quantity produced amounted, in 1834, to only 101 tons, valued at £2,849.—There were five mines from which manganese is obtained. The produce of these was 1,039 tons, valued at £4,206. Saline substances.] Under the head of saline substances are included common salt (chloride of sodium), copperas (sulphate of iron), alum (sulphate of alumina and potass). The first of these substances, common salt, is procured chiefly from the evaporation of sea-water, but partly also from mines of fossil salt, and saline springs impregnated by fossil salt. The proportions in which salt is procured from these different sources are,— From evaporation of sea-water, .905 From brine springs, 058 From mines of fossil salt, .037 The total produce of these in 1842 was 407,250 tons, value £568,351. This quantity was found to be sufficient for the consumption of the country, and to leave upwards of 50,000 tons for exportation, principally to Sardinia and Switzerland. The strongest of the great salt-springs at Salins yields 23 per cent. of salt. There are salt-springs at Dieuse, and Chateau-Salies in Lorraine; and salt-refineries at Moyenvic, in the district of the Three bishoprics, as it is F. coast; particularly in Brittany, Saintonge, Aunis, Normandy, Poitou, and Languedoc. The number of mines in operation, from which the aluminous and pyritic substances of which copperas and alum are made, was 16 in 1842. The produce of alum in the year 1834 was 2,330 tons, valued at £33,228; the quantity of copperas, 3,177 tons, valued at £12,765. In 1842, 61 salt-marshes, a mine of rock salt, 13 salt-springs, &c., produced 4,077,291 metrical quintals of salt, valued at 14,889,451 francs, and occupied 10,894 persons. The hyacinth, emerald, beryl, tourmaline, and amethyst, occur in different parts of F., as also the chalcedony in the department of the Isere. Turquoises scarcely inferior to those of the East, are among the fossil productions of the mountains of the Rouergue. Porcelain earth is found at Limoges. Chalk and limestone abound; and gypsum, or plaster of Paris, is met with in great abundance in the vicinity of the capital, especially at Montmartre. Besides the excellent quarries of freestone, in the vicinity of Paris, there are many others in the kingdom. These quarries afford hard and solid stones of surprising magnitude. Quarries of a kind of jasper are found near Salins, some blocks from which are so large as to be capable of making columns from 12 to 15 ft. high. III. * Marble quarries abound in the Pyrenees. Flint is also plentiful. Value of metallic produce.] The metallic produce of F. during the year 1842 was-cast-iron, 3,994,557 metrical quintals; bar-iron, 2,848,238; copper, 1,885; silver, 2,374; lead, 6,321; antimony, 190,705; and manganese, 116,150. The total value of the different branches of the mining industry was stated to have been 404,496,704 francs, viz., coal and turf, 38,823,963 francs; the manufacturing of iron and steel, 148,074,900 francs; other metals, besides iron, mineral bitumen, and salt, 18,171,045 francs; working of quarries, 41,047,519 francs; ditto of substances of mineral origin, 158,379,277 francs. The mineral industry occupied 313,155 workmen. Mineral springs.] The principal mineral waters in F. are those of Aix, Bareges, and Bagneres-de-Luchen. The springs of Aix were known to the Romans: baths having been established here by C. Sextius Calvinius. The temp. of the water is about the same with that of the Queen's bath at Bath; and, in its component parts, it is similar to that of Aix-la- Chapelle: viz., sulphur, carbonate of lime, and muriate of soda. Two of the tepid springs at Bagneres are equal to the temperature of the human body; 10 are above it, and 18 below it. The other mineral springs of any consequence in F. are those of Forges, Vichy, Bourbon-Lancy, Balaruc, Plombieres, and Passy. The greater part of these are under the inspection and superintendence of physicians appointed by government. Vegetable productions.] The total number of vegetable species indigenous to or naturalized in F. was estimated in the Botanicon Gallicum of M. Duby, in 1828, at 7,276. Of phanerogamous plants, F. possesses 2,950 dicotyledonous, out of 64,600 the total number known; and 713 monocotyledonous out of 15,400. Of cryptogamous plants, F. has 75 vascular cryptogams out of 4,500 known; and 3,423 celcular, out of 10,500 known. The following comparative table of the phanerogamous plants indigenous in the different states of Europe is from Berghaus' Physical Atlas: 3,660 France, Lapland, . . . 495 Germany, 407 2,280 Iceland, . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . Iceland, Switzerland, 2,255 Denmark, 1,035 N. of Italy, 2,015 . . . Scotland, . Greece, Prussia, The most numerous families of plants in F. are the Compositæ, numbering 457; the Leguminaceæ, 343; the Graminaceæ, 267; the Cruciferæ, 202; the Caryophyleaceæ, 152; the Labratæ, 47; and the Ranunculaceæ, 130. The most universally diffused genera are Carex, Veronica Galium, Trifolium, Euphorbia, Orches, Poa, Juncus, Ranunculus, Chenopodium, Bromus, and Salix. Animal kingdom.] The zoology of F. so far as regards the domestic animals will be given in a subsequent paragraph. Bears occur in the neighbourhood of the Pyrenees, and in the Alpine districts of Dauphiné. There are two species of bears known here,—the carnivorous and the graminivorous. The latter, though not so fierce as the former, are yet more mischievous,—coming down in the night, and eating the corn, particularly buck-wheat and maize, and trampling and spoiling more than they eat .- Wolves are still numerous in different parts of F., as in Morbihan, Sarthe, Vendée, and the central deps., and are very destructive to the sheep. A very large wolf infested the wood of Orleans for several years, in the middle of the last cent.; and was so bold and ferocious as to issue out of the wood by night and carry-off women and children from the neighbouring * villages. Wild boars are found in different parts of the kingdom. The red and fallow deer have become rare. The ibex and chamois are found in the Pyrenees and the Alps of Dauphine; foxes, otters, wild-cats, martins, and squirrels, are common in different districts; and the beaver is said to occur in the islands at the mouth of the Rhone.—The eagle, flamingo, wasp-eater, ortolan, bustard, red and grey partridges, and becafria, occur in F. ornithology.- Scorpions are common in the southern provinces. Vipers abound in La Vendée. In some deps. frogs are reared as an article of food. Leeches are exported in large quantities.—Bees are numerous; and honey is a very general product. That of Narbonne is esteemed the best. Bleaching wax is an important business in F.; and several manufactories for that purpose are established in different places, particularly at Montpellier. Agriculture.] Louis XIV., in prescribing to the intendants of provinces to collect together materials with which to form the general statistics of the kingdom, especially recommended them from which we are a century and a half distant,—says a French official paper,-nothing was prepared for such vast and difficult investigations. The maps which represented the territory differed from each other 5,818 mean leagues in the extent, or nearly 1 in 4. There was no register of the physical and agricultural division of the country; the impost, which varied from one province to another both in form and quota, gave no light of which use could be made to estimate, even by approximation, the quantity and value of the natural productions on which it was levied. The censuses of the pop., without which it is impossible to form · a just idea of the consumption, were imperfect and defective. They were made according to the district, either from the number of families or the number of hearths; and there was no fixed rule of agreement as to the number of individuals that each method must necessarily include. The opinions upon this subject differed so much as to increase or diminish the pop. by nearly one-fifth; which left an uncertainty of 20 per cent, in all that concerned agricultural production. Vauban had recourse to a method which seems strange to us in the present day, but which was then ingenious. Accustomed by the science of war to calculation and observation, he made a minute topographical investigation of some parts of the western provs., and he determined what was the mean extent of each species of surface in a territory of a sq. league of 25 to the degree. He came to the conclusion that there was in this extent- Arpens Hectares. communaux. 600 in wood, . From these data, applied to the entire surface of the kingdom, Vauban supposed the territory of F. to be distributed in the following manner :- Hectares. Sq. leagues. Roads and rivers, . .. ... Commons, waste, or bar- Total surface, These numbers were not by any means, as has been frequently supposed, correct arithmetical calculations, explaining the real state of the country; they had no foundation but in bold conjecture. A skilful English agriculturist, Arthur Young, who travelled over F. in 1788, having searched in vain, in the documents of that period, for numbers expressing the extent of the different parts of the territory, divided according to its physical and agriextent of surface which it represented, he determined the proportion of these two terms, and the arithmetical number of each particular weight gave him that of each species of surface.—The committee of the National assembly, charged in 1790 to prepare the establishment of a system of taxation upon a rational basis, not finding in the archives of the kingdom the positive data which they required, had recourse to the assistance of Lavoisier, who, having been one of the farmers-general, must have elaborated with the advantages of a superior mind all the statistical notions which were then possessed on this important matter. The writing which the committee received in reply is a curious document, and may be considered as giving in an official manner the state of economical science, as respects F., at the end of the last century. This document proceeds in the following manner in what relates to the agricultural statistics of the kingdom. He lays down, in the first place, as a principal fact, an accessory which would now be altogether neglected, and which we cannot see without surprise adopted as the foundation of all his calculations,-namely the number of ploughs. He proves that there were then in F.: 320,000 worked by horses. 600,000 oxen. 920,000 Each plough- Hect Arpens. Worked by horses, ploughed in autumn, Of these numbers it was concluded that there was annually: Hect. Arpens. 9,600,000 or 4,902,910 cultivated by horses in autumn. It was supposed that the fallow ground was exactly of the extent of the land cultivated in autumn, and that there was consequently,- Arpens. Hect. 9,600,000 or 4,902,910 in the districts cultivated by horses. 9,000,000 4,596,480 in those cultivated by oxen. 8,499,490 in fallow ground. 18,600,000 It was admitted, moreover, that there was in the districts cultivated by oxen an extent of ground in unprofitable pasture double that of the fallow ground, viz., Hect. Arpens. 18,000,000 or 9,193,000 in sheep walks and commons. These united numbers led to the belief that there was: Hect. Arpens. 28,200,000 or 14,402,300 In corn land, . . In fallow ground, . . . 18,600,000 In examining these statistical returns, we find that not one of them was the result of local investigations, and that they were nothing but a chain of deductions drawn from the hypothesis that it was possible to know the extent of arable land by the exact number of its ploughs. More recent investigations, and calculations founded on better data, inform us that the soil of F. is thus divided: Rich soil, . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 7,276,368 hect. Gravelly soil, 3,417,898 Stony, 6,621,348 Sandy, 5,912,377 Clay, 2,232,885 Marshy, 284,454 Varied Mountainous, and unfit for cultivation, 4,268,750 Thus, only about a seventh is really good land. But the agriculturists divide the land into five classes. The department of the North, the Limagne in Auvergne, the valley of the Isère, the plain of Meaux, with portions of Alsace, equal any known land. Thus about 4 deps. are good, 23 middling, 16 passable, 25 mediocre, while there is a space equal to 18 deps. of bad land, roads, buildings, rivers, and wholly barren. The employment of this surface, according to another estimate, was in round numbers as follows: Arable land, Canals, Cultures particulieres, or crops too small to be classed but as sundries, . Total. The English acre is to the French hectare as 0,4049 to 1,000, or nearly 2 to 5. The Marne is the dep. possessing the greatest amount of arable land, viz., 614,000 hectares; the Cantal is the richest in pasture, of which it comprises 221,000 hect.; the greatest extent of vineyards is found in the Gironde, viz., 138,000 hect.; the most extensive woods are in the dep. of the Nievre, viz., 239,000 hect., or more than one-third of its whole superficies. Progress of agriculture.] Previous to the first revolution, the state of agriculture in F. was little superior to that of any other country of continental Europe. Nearly two-fifths of the land susceptible of cultivation were in what is termed culture and pasturage, and produced on an average about one-half of what good culture on the like quantity of the same soil would have produced. The various tenures under which land was at this period held were almost all decidedly injurious to agriculture. The manorrents of the clergy have been variously estimated: by Condorcet, their landed revenue was calculated at nearly one-fifth of the property of the kingdom; Neckar estimated their revenue at 130,000,000 livres. It is at any rate probable, that the least valuation of their manorial rental amounted to 120,000,000 livres, or £4,800,000 sterling, independent of their tithes, which might be estimated at £3,600,000 sterling. The domains of the Crown, and of the princes of the blood, yielded a rental of £1,200,000 sterling. The honorary and feudal dues paid to the nobility (whose numbers exceeded 44,000), with corvées, militia, &c., amounted at least to £5,000,000 sterling; and the government drew from the produce of agriculture the sum of £8,000,000 sterling. In fine, it has been calculated, that, exclusive of the rents of land paid to the lay proprietors, and of the duties of excise, consumption, and the like, the produce of the soil was charged annually with upwards of £21,000,000 sterling. But disadvantages still more discouraging and oppressive pressed upon agriculture previous to the revolution, which we cannot properly understand and estimate without a due consideration of the different modes of occupying land which then existed, and some of which still remain. In the first place, there were the small properties of the peasants. These were numerous to a degree of which we have no conception in Britain, and which we should not have expected in the midst of the enormous possessions and oppressive privileges of the nobility and clergy; and were to be found even in those provs, where other tenures prevailed, but principally in Languedoc, in the dep. of the Lot, the whole of the district of the Pyrenees, Bearn, Gascony, part of Guyenne, Alsace, Flanders, and Lorraine. The condition of the peasantry who possessed these small properties varied much in different Parts of the kingdom. In Flanders, Alsace, on the Garonne, and and might rather be denominated farmers than cottagers; in Lower Brittany many of them were rich; but this character could by no means apply to them generally. The minute division of property had, in fact, produced the effects which might be exvisible, particularly in Lorraine and the adjacent districts of Champagne. A rental in money was the second mode of possessing land. These tenures, though also found in other parts of F., did not exist upon a moderate estimate in more than a 6th or 7th part of the kingdom before the revolution. The mode of occupying land was by feudal tenures. This mode prevailed most in Brittany, Limousin, Berry, and La Manche, but was in use in a greater or less degree throughout the whole kingdom. These feudal tenures were fiefs, granted by the seigneurs or lords of the parishes, under a reservation of fines, quitrents, forfeitures, services, &c., many of which were of the most oppressive kind. Nay, even the landed proprietors themselves were greatly harassed by the capitaineries or paramountship of certain districts, granted by the king to princes of the blood; putting them in possession of the property of all game even on lands not their own, and on manors granted long before to individuals; so that by this paramountship all manorial rights were annihilated. That species of oppression denominated the corvees was also peculiarly oppressive and injurious to agriculture, though not confined to the feudal tenures just mentioned. By the corvees individuals were obliged to mend the roads by personal labour. It is evident that this tax must have fallen exclusively on the poor; or if it was performed by those who kept labourers, it must have deprived them of the means of attending fully to their agricultural occupations. It was also an easy engine of oppression; for, under the pretence of carrying on the work without interruption, those who were liable to the corvee had it frequently allotted them at some leagues from their dwelling. There were also military corvees, which fell only on the villages situated in the line of march, the inhabitants of which were obliged to leave their occupation, however inconvenient and injurious it might be, and repair the roads with the of money hired great tracts of land at a money-rent, and relet them in small divisions to metayers. This mode of occupying land was most common in Poitou, Berry, La-Manche, and Augoumois; but it also occurred in other provs. The metayers were a kind of farmers that gradually succeeded to the slave cultivators or coloni partiarii of the Romans. They supplied the labour necessary for cultivating the land, while the proprietor furnished them with the seed, cattle, and implements of husbandry, and in short the whole stock necessary for cultivating the farm. It was the common agreement between the proprietor and the farmer that the produce should be equally divided, after setting aside what was necessary for keeping up the stock, which was restored to the proprietor when the tenant quitted his farm. Seveneighths of the land in F. were held under this tenure before the Revolution. Half the produce was generally paid to the proprietor; but in Champagne it seldom exceeded a third. The metayers were so poor in some parts of F., and consequently so dependent on their landlords, that they were obliged almost every year to borrow corn for bread from them before the harvest came round. Such were the tenures of land before the Revolution. Let us now see what effect that event has produced on them, and on the condition of the agricultural class in general. In the first place, the number of small properties has been greatly increased in all parts of F. The national domains, consisting of the confiscated estates of the church and emigrant nobility, were exposed to sale during the pecuniary distresses of the revolutionary government. For the accommodation of the lowest order of purchasers, they were divided into small portions, and 5 years were allowed for completing the payment. In consequence of this indulgence, and of the depreciation of assignats, the poorest classes of the peasantry were enabled to become proprietors of from 1 to 10 acres. Another cause has also contributed to increase the number of small proprietors since the Revolution. Before that event it seems to have been the law, or at least the custom, invariably to divide the landed property among all the children. This originally local law was extended soon after the Revolution to the whole kingdom; so that, by the present law of F. abolishing the right of primogeniture in landed property, the largest estate must sooner or later be broken up, and the succession to the smallest patch regulated by the principle of equal division among the children. The deplorable result of this division and subdivision of little properties, in a country like F., already so fully appropriated, may be easily conceived. Division of property.] Perhaps the best illustration of the state to which landed property is tending in F. may be deduced from the returns to the contribution fonciere. It appears from the tables given in a pamphlet of the Duc de Gaete, published in 1818, that there were in 1816 no fewer than 10,414,121 taxable properties great and small. And in an official report we find it stated that there were in 1834 no less than Francs. 5,205,411 properties paying a land-tax of less than 5 between 5 and *** ... 1,000 and upwds. 13,361 ... ... 10,893,528 From this table it would appear that there were upwards of 10,000,000 of landed proprietors in F., each owning on an average about 2 acres; but it must be borne in mind that several properties frequently belong to one proprietor, and it would therefore be impossible, from the foregoing table, to make any estimate approaching to accuracy of the real number of landowners in F. M. de Gaete reckoned that there were in 1818, 4,833,000 individual proprietors, of whom 3,665,300 paid upon an average 12.88 francs yearly tax upon their property, representing an yearly income of 64 francs, or 51s. sterling, being in fact mere day-labourers with a cottage and garden belonging to themselves. "It appears," says a writer in the Quarterly Review, "by the Documens Statistiques, published by the French government in 1835, that there existed on the 1st Sept. 1834, no less than 123,360,338 different lots or parcelles of land, each a distinct division on the cadastre of something less on the average than an English acre or the cultivable soil. This fact, however, though it evidences an almost incredible subdivision of the soil, is, it is admitted, no index to the present state of property. In the first place, it will be observed that houses and buildings, of which fifty might stand on an acre, are each reckoned as at least one parcelle, and there are 7,000,000 of them, with, we presume, their subdivisions, to be deducted from the gross number, leaving of merely territorial parcelles perhaps about 110,000,000. But the number of these parcelles is of no great importance to our present inquiry, because though once separate, or derived under different titles, they have been frequently and extensively reunited in the same hands; and the real question is as to the number, not of properties, but of proprietors. But that unluckily cannot be ascertained with any degree of precision, from the impossibility, as stated by the ministerial reports, of discovering how many of those minute parcelles, scattered over the face of F., and under a constant process do, possess land in more than one commune, 'those who,' say our authors, 'have studied the subject, have arrived at the conclusion that there are about 5,500,000 (5,446,763) distinct proprietary families in F.' We find in the table of communal rates, that there are no less than 5,163,000 proprietors rated at less than 5 francs = 4s.; and the average of the contribution foncière for the whole of F. being 21 francs per hectare, equal to 10d. the English acre, we have thus upwards of 5,000,000 of proprietors whose average holdings would be under 5 acres, and a great majority of course considerably under; and there are 3,300,000 other communal proprietors, whose taxes indicate their possessions to be on an average under 10 acres. We should not a priori have thought it likely that there were amongst these small proprietors any great number holding property in different communes, and we are therefore led to suspect that the calculation of 5,500,000 of distinct proprietors is at all events not exaggerated. Assuming it then to be just, our surprise at even that number is not diminished by the consequences that follow. In the first place, it appears from the official returns that the whole value of real property (immeuables) of F. is estimated at 39,515,000,000 francs, equal to about £1,580,000,000 sterling; and its annual revenue at 1,580,000,000 francs, about £63,000,000 sterling,—about 4 per cent; so that the average income of the 5,500,000 proprietors would be about 287 francs, equal to £11 10s. sterling; but this average, low as it is, gives a much more favourable view of the case than the following more detailed estimate of the several classes of income, which, reduced into pounds sterling, will, we think, surprise our English readers: 2,602,705 families whose revenue does not exceed £2 *** ... 6,681 families whose revenues exceed 400 5,446,763 " Produce of grain.] It is stated by M. Millot that in 1830 the medium return of wheat for the seed sown, taking the average of all F., was about 5.2 times. The mean produce was 12 hectolitres, or 33 bushels, per hectare, or 2.4712 acres. The maximum produce was 20 hectolitres, or 55 bushels, per hectare; the minimum, 4.62 hectolitres, or 12.8 bushels. M. Millot calculates the produce in England, Scotland, and Germany to be 40 hectolitres per hectare, which is 44 bushels per acre; and, in doing so, he probably refers to the maximum produce. Since that time, however, he states that some further progress has been made in French agriculture; and that, on a comparison of 1845 with 1815, the following has been the rate of increase:-Population had increased from 29,000,000 to 35,000,000. The quantity of land employed in growing wheat (estimated for each inhabitant), from 0.157 to 0.166 hectares. The produce per hectare, from 1,245 to 1,345 litres; yielding for each inhabitant 223 litres, or 392.7 pints; and for each inhabitant, by weight, 161.5 kilogrammes, or 356 lbs. From this last item, deducting the reserve for seed, distillation, &c., estimated at 26.5 kilog., there were left as food for each inhabitant, 135 kilog., or 297.5 lbs., in place of 119.75 kilog. in 1815. The last proportion, or increase in the weight of food per head, gives a gain of 121 per cent.; to which adding about 9 per cent., which M. Millot estimates is the amount of improvement in grinding and baking this wheat into bread, he obtains an increase of 211 per cent. in the wheat-bread consumed in F., on a comparison of 1845 with 1815. The increase in the pop., during the same period, was about 201 per cent. The consumption of wheat in F., at the different periods under-mentioned, was estimated by different authorities as follows: Years. Total quantity. Each inhabitant. Litres. Hectol. 100 [Vauban] 118 Annuaire de l'Eco-<br>nomie for 1851. Thus in one century and a half, the pop. of F. is not double, yet the consumption of corn has more than tripled. Since 1815 F. has annually imported, on an average, 800,000 hectolitres of wheat, representing a value of more than 17,500,000 francs. During a period of 30 years, dating from 1815, the production of wheat not being sufficient for public consumption, it was necessary for 20 successive harvests to import foreign corn. The quantity imported exceeded at one time more than 4,000,000 hectolitres, namely in 1832; three times, in 1840, 1842, and 1843, it exceeded 2,000,000; and three times it exceeded 1,000,000 hectolitres. During the 10 other years the importation was insignificant, or under 66,000 hectolitres. It thus appears that the cereal portion of two out of every three harvests in F. is insufficient for the internal consumption of the country, and this part of the general produce of the soil constitutes considerably more than half the food of the nation, according to M. de Gasparin, who represents the proportions of the different kinds of food that compose the provisions of F., the whole being taken at 100, as follows: Cereal grain, It would appear from the above statements that in F. the pop. presses upon the means of subsistence, yet the amount of agricultural production in F. has not only vastly increased in recent years, but bears favourable comparison with that of any other country in Europe. "The production of wheat," says an article in the Annuaire de l'Economie et de la Statistique for 1850, "has increased in the ratio of more than 1 grain in 6. In 1790, according to Lavoisier, it was not more than 5 to 1. It is now 6.07, calculating all the different products of the 36,000 communes, according to a rigorous method that is not in usage in any other country. Corn, without distinction of kinds, now gives 13 hectolitres, 14 litres per hectare, instead of 8, as in 1733. This production gave to each inhabitant, in 1760, 450 litres; it now gives 541, and yet the pop. has increased by 13,000,000 in 86 years. The wheat harvests yield on an average more than 70,000,000 of hectolitres. In 1784 it was only 40,000,000. Every person has 208 litres of this at present, instead of 167 as then. There is not any known consumption so great in Europe. It is: In France per inhabitant, . . . 208 litres. Great Britain and Ireland, . 163 Spain, . 127 . . Austria, Holland and Belgium, Prussia, Poland, 25 Sweden, "This same superiority which we have over the principal countries in Europe, we find progressively acquired by our forefathers, whose consumption of wheat amounted to only one-third 27 years ago. The consumption was: 118 litres for each inhabitant. In 1700, under Louis XIV., 1840, in present France, "But if we admit, with more truth, that the consumption is unequal, and that those who are nourished by wheat only constitute one more or less numerous part of the population, we find the following explanation of this interesting fact. In 1700, during the brilliant reign of Louis XIV., 39 inhabitants out of 100 lived upon white bread, and 61 on black bread, made with inferior grain. In 1784, under Louis XVI., at the height of the prosperity of the monarchy, instead of 39 there were 41 inhabitants who were fed with wheat. Scarcely any progress had taken place. At the present time the proportion is 60 out of 100, and the proportion of the pop. consuming wheat is vastly greater than that of those who subsist on inferior grain. This is the reverse of what took place under Louis XIV. "It may indeed be objected that 14,000,000 of inhabitants are still reduced to accept the inferior cereals as an aliment, and that in this respect England is far in advance of this country. Truly only white bread is eaten in that country; but when it fails, the people starve, passing at once from luxury to famine. Rather than this, it were better, we think, to be content with barley bread, or even the buckwheat cake. An increase of one-half in 60 years guarantees that from now to the end of the cent., all our pop. will have the advantage of consuming wheat. F. possesses at the present day, in the middle of the 19th cent., an agricultural production, which, for quality and quantity of its results, is incomparably superior to that of any other country of Europe, and of any other epoch in the economic history of modern times. Russia which, like the Egypt of former days, passes for being the granary of famished populations, produces from a sown hectare of wheat 2 hectolitres less than the lands of F. Lombardy, Belgium, and England do not yield for equal acres a greater return of wheat than the best of our departments. Their apparent superiority arises from the restricted extent of their territories; and if we add Scotland and Ireland to England, the average of production is reduced just as it is for France when we add the deps. of Lozere, Correze, the Hautes, and Basses Alpes, and other infertile districts. In no other region of the world, and in the annals of no other people, do we meet with an instance of agricultural production in the space of 44 years having increased from 70,000,000 of hectolitres of grain to 183,000,000; or, in other words, which has increased threefold in the like period." Rents and farms.] The rent of land is low in most parts of F. Before the first Revolution, the rent of the land, both arable and in lucerne, was averaged by Mr. Young at 15s. 7d., woods at 12s., vineyards at £3 16s. 6d., meadows at £2 3s. 3d., and wastes at 1s. 9d. per acre; good land may be got at present at 22s. and 26s. the acre. The land-tax of F. is thus organised. Its amount is 5 per cent., and the method of levying it is so arranged as, without going into unwieldy and impracticable details, to get, with average fairness, at an estimate of the actual value of the produce of the land. The tax is, of course, estimated upon the value of the net profit of the produce in question after all expenses, the interest of capital included, have been paid. The revenue upon which the tax is laid would, therefore, in fact, were the land let out, amount to nearly the rent which would be paid for it. In metairies—the only species of tenure approaching to farm tenancy known in the South-the landlord pays all public burdens; the occupying tenant has nothing to do with any of them. Public railways, and great rivers used as common tracks of navigation, are esteemed public property, and are exempted from taxation. The burden of supporting local roads in France does not exclusively fall upon the landed property of the district through which they run. Every male individual, between the ages of eighteen and sixty, is bound to give, either in kind or in money, three days' labour upon the roads of his department, and that whether he inhabits town or country. [Morning Chronicle.] The French farms are generally small in size,-few, even in Normandy and the other provs. where agriculture is conducted on the best plan and with the greatest capital and skill, reaching 300 acres. As to agricultural capital, Young estimated it at 40s. per acre, employed on the farms, on an average of all the kingdom. It appears, however, that the average is at present much higher. The chief defect in French husbandry seems to consist in attempting to have corn upon the land as frequently as possible. The consequence is, that few cattle are fed, manure is wanting, and the soil becomes exhausted. Where maize can be cultivated it supplies the place of fallow; and then the ground, instead of lying waste, affords a crop useful for human sustenance as well as for the rearing of cattle. Beans are cultivated in some places, but much less universally than might be proper. In several districts, broom is permitted at certain times to overrun the land. When tender, it is applied to the purpose of feeding cattle; but the greater part is made into faggots for fuel. Chiccory, a green crop, little known in England, has been cultivated in some districts of F. to much advantage. Turnips are almost wholly unknown as objects of culture; and where introduced, are frequently not very properly managed. Tillage in some places is neatly executed; but, in the greater part of the country, it is performed in a very slovenly manner. In many parts of F., they still tread out the corn with oxen, horses, or mules, instead of thrashing it by a manual operation, or by machine. Great part of the country is enclosed, but the fences too often bespeak the inattention of the farmer. "In five-sixths of F.," says Baron Dupin, in his Petit Producteur, "the agricultural instruments are still made in the rudest form. They are so badly constructed, so ill adapted to the animal power which sets them in motion, that they cause the half of it, two-thirds, and sometimes even three-fourths, to be wasted. There are still some parts of F. where the people have not a sufficient number of domestic animals to prevent the women being employed as beasts of burden or draught; they drag barrows and dung-carts, haul along boats and vessels, carry heavy burdens, drive the plough, and share the most irksome labours." "You look in vain," says a writer in the Morning Chronicle, recording the results of his impressions and inquiries in 1850, "you look in vain, in the corn-lands of F., for the neatly-constructed and ingeniously-adapted mechanical contrivances for drilling, clod-crushing, sowing, and so forth, more or less in use over every acre of grain-growing land in England. In the course of two days' rambling about Mantes, I saw nothing further advanced than very small, and some of them very rude, wheeled ploughs, and light harrows with wooden teeth. The forks with which the people were turning the black trefoil hay in the fields were merely cloven sticks, and like implements were in use in the barns and barn-yards. The soil about Mantes is very light and sandy, and a horse of the pot-bellied breed, so common in France, assisted by a very well-kept donkey, seems amply sufficient to do all the pulling required at the plough. The truth is, however, that for all the depth to which the ploughshare penetrates, the donkey, as it struck me, could have very nearly performed the business himself. To all intents and purposes it was scratching rather than ploughing; and as I watched the progress of the shallow furrows, I thought of Mr. Mechi's declaration, that England was not ploughed one-third deep enough, and wondered what the agriculturist of Tiptree-hall would have said to the sun-burnt honest-faced French peasant contentedly whistling between his short plough stilts. The ploughs in question consist mainly of a stout block of wood forming the body, and to which the small and inefficient share is attached; wheels are invariably added, to no good purpose, as any one acquainted with the working and principle of the instrument knows, while the shortness of the stilts yield only a slight and insufficient leverage power to the conductor. In barns and farm-yards the primitive flail was, as might be expected, in use, instead of the threshing-mill. A still ruder process was going on in one grange into which I adventured. The operator took the corn-sheaves by handfuls and beat their heads across a wooden tressel. The process was slow, but it was, as I understood, preferred to that of the flail, inasmuch as the straw was less broken and spoiled by the operation. Except in particular districts of F., it is well known that the small proprictors principally reside in clustered villages, or in the suburbs of neighbouring towns, from whence they come every day to their labours in the fields. Such a village, exclusively occupied by the neighbouring landowners-how different is the French from the English ordinary acceptation of the word!-I explored near Mantes. It was a jumble of rudely built houses, mostly thatched, and each surrounded by its appertaining farm-buildings; in other words, by perhaps a tumble-down looking shed, built of unhewn beams and clay, within which a roughly put together cart, and half-a-dozen spades, scythes, and so forth would be bestowed, from under the shelter of which would be heard proceeding the quick heavy beat of the flail, and conducting through its wide open portals to a yard thickly strewn with the refuse of the stable and the byre-masses of trampled fodder and dung, distilling pools of liquid manure, which oozed into and stunk from the lower nooks and corners. The house, barn, shed, stables, and so forth, are thus all crowded together, and connected round the yard in the centre-spaces being, however, sometimes left between each establishment, and devoted mainly to the growing of grapes for the table, luxuriant clusters of the fruit twirling round the copings of the white clay walls, and hanging temptingly over the rudely-paved lane beneath. The village consisted of a jumble of such erections, half dwelling-places, half farm-buildings, intersected by devious lanes, and by the uneven and irregularly running walls upon which the grapes were trained. There was only a single shop, a debit du tabac, and no publichouse. Why should there be, in fact? Every man, as a general rule, lived upon his own ground, and had his own wine in his own cellar. The aspect of things was certainly rude and uncultivated; neatness, order, a sense of cleanliness, and the ability to make a better disposition of the space at the command of the inhabitants, were all wanting; but of extreme poverty and squalor there were not the remotest indications. These farmers might cultivate their small patches in the most unskilful and slovenly fashion, but it was easy to see that they cultivated them so as to make a fair living out of the soil." Horticulture.] The French have made great progress in the useful branches of horticulture. The banks of the Moselle, Finistère in Normandy, the environs of Lyons and of Aix, are all equally celebrated for their horticultural industry. The fruitgardens at Montreuil, near Paris, are a curious instance of the accumulation of capital in a small space: they are said to be worth £400 an acre; and all the occupants are proprietors. The environs of Lyons are celebrated for their artichokes, which are carefully conveyed in great quantities all over the kingdom; vegetables for the table are also cultivated in great perfection at Provence, and particularly about Aix. The inhabitants of Roscoff, a town on the N point of the dep. of Finistère, apply themselves particularly to raising vegetables for the table, and are so eminently successful in this branch, that Brest, Morlaix, and several other towns, draw their supplies almost entirely from them. Cauliflowers, broccoli, cabbages, turnips, asparagus, and artichokes, are produced here in amazing abundance, and of an excellent quality. The value of the annual produce of the orchards in F. is estimated at 21,500,000 francs; that of the fruit cultivated by espaliers at 64,500,000 f.; and that of the kitchen-gardens at 197,000,000 f. In the department of ornamental gardening, F. is still far behind: the straight avenue, the terrace, the parterre, with formal basins and jets d'eau, are still the only objects which, in the opinion of the generality of Frenchmen, constitute real grandeur and beauty in a garden. Vineyards.] As wine is one of the most valuable products of F., one might have expected, from the aides and customs levied on the consumption and export of this article previous to the Revolution, that the extent of vineyards in the kingdom might have been estimated with a tolerable degree of accuracy. Yet this is by no means the case. M. de Trosne, about the year 1780, assigned them an extent of 1,600,000 arpens. The author of the Crédit National, supposed them at the same period to occupy nearly 8 times that space; Mr. Young concluded that the culture of the vine occupied nearly the 26th part of the territory of F.; and the justness of this conjecture was confirmed by M. Chaptal, who gave the extent of the vineyards at 1,977,000 hectares, or were returned at 1,960,755; in 1849 at 2,137,000. The vine is cultivated on all kinds of soil; but is mostly planted on rocky and inferior soils, in all situations and exposures. In the beginning of the last cent., upon an average of five years, from the year 1720 to 1725, F. exported annually, wine to the amount of 20,880,000 livres. In 1778, the exportation amounted to 24,570,170 liv.; and it appears from the official returns that the value of the wines sent from France to foreign countries, on an average of the three years ending with 1790, amounted to 32,368,500 francs, and that of the brandies, vinegars, &cs, to 18,627,600 f., making together nearly 51,000,000 f., or about £2,000,000 sterling. But, notwithstanding the increased opulence and growing taste for French wines in the N of Europe, and America, and their increased consumption in this country since the reduction of the duties in 1824, such has been the influence of the prohibitory system, or of the exclusion from F. of the principal equivalents that foreigners had to give in exchange for wines, that their export is less than what it amounted to previously to 1790, and a large proportion of the actual exports are made on speculation only. Nay, the home-traders are oppressed in a grievous manner by fiscal and custom-house regulations. The wine-growers of F. cannot convey their wine from one cellar to another in the same establishment without payment of a duty, or introduce it into any town but it must pay a duty or octroi at the barrier, which increases according to the pop. of the town. A tun of wine which has cost the purchaser 60 francs, would, if introduced into Bourdeaux, be charged with a duty of 97 f. or 161 per cent.; and if into Paris, it would have to pay no less than 220 f. or 366 per cent. The duties of all kinds levied on wines in 1846 amounted to 59,176,465 f.; and the quantity of wine produced was estimated at 45,000,000 hectolitres.—Whilst England consumes only a 4 gallon of wine per head, the consumption of F. is nearly 20 gallons per head annually. The French at present deservedly rank as the best wine-makers in the world.-The Champagne, Dr. Henderson informs us, is divided into the river and mountain wines; the former being for the most part white, and the latter red. By Champagne wine is generally understood a sparkling or frothing liquor. The district, however, furnishes many wines which do not effervesce; but those which effervesce slightly are the best. Among the white wines of Champagne, the first rank is usually assigned to those of Sillery, comprehending the produce of those vineyards which are situate at the NE termination of the chain of hills that separates the Marne from the Vesle. It is a dry still liquor, of a light water colour, with considerable body, and a flavour somewhat analogous to that of the first growths of the Rhine. In general, it may be observed, that the vineyards on the banks of the Marne supply the best river-wines, and that they degenerate in proportion to the distance from that river; though this rule is not without exceptions. The vintages of Hautvilliers and Ay are the most esteemed. The price of vineyard land is very high, and has increased. An arpent, which is little more than an English acre, and which is estimated to produce 800 bottles, sells as high as about £600 or £700. Champagne wines, when placed in cool cellars, will retain their good qualities from 10 to 20 years. The vaults in which the stock is kept in France are excavated out of rock to the depth of 30 or 40 ft., where Fahrenheit's thermometer stands at 54°, and varies from winter to summer only An official report, made by the directors of the indirect taxes for the dep. of the Marne, furnishes the following information as to the trade in champagne between the 1st of April, 1846, and the 1st of April, 1847. The produce is divided between the arrondissements of Chalons, Epernay, and Reims. On the 1st of April, 1847, there were, in the cellars of the wholesale dealers, 18,815,367 bottles of this wine, viz., Chalons, 4,604,237 bottles; at Epernay, 5,710,753; and at Reims, 8,500,377. The number of bottles sold and delivered between the 1st of April, 1846, and the 1st of April, 1847, were, at Chalons, 2,497,355; at Epernay. 2,187,553; at Reims, 4,090,577; making a total of 8,775,485 bottles. These quantities were thus distributed:— To go abroad. To other deps. Within the dep. From Chalons, Thus twice as much champagne wine is sent abroad as is consumed in France.—The wines of Burgundy are produced in the greatest excellence in the deps. of the Cote-d'Or, Yonne, and Saone and Loire, or in Upper and Lower Burgundy. The choice red growths in the Cote-d'Or are the Romanee Conti, Clos-Vougeot, Chambertin, Richebourg, Romanée-de-Saint-Vivant, Tache, and St. George. These, according to Mr. Henderson, "are distinguished by their beautiful colour and exquisite flavour and perfume, combining in a greater degree than any other wines the qualities of lightness and delicacy with richness and fulness of body." The vineyards of Musigny, Clos-de-Premeau, Clos-du- Tart, Veroilles. Clos-Morjot, Clos-St.-Jean, and Perriere, also furnish wines of nearly equal excellence; but the quantity being very limited they are little known out of Burgundy. The white wines of Burgundy are less numerous than the red, but maintain nevertheless the highest rank among the French white wines, and are not inferior to the red either in flavour or perfume. Two leagues and a half to the SE of Beaune is grown the famous Mont Rachet wine, surpassing all the other white wines of the Cote-d'Or, by its high perfume and agreeable nutty flavour. The choicest growths of Burgundy are very difficult to be procured, and are almost all consumed in France. Some wine obtained by M. Montmort from his vineyard at Fissin, near Dijon, which is said to surpass all the other growths of the Cote-d'Or, has been sold on the spot at the enormous price of 12 francs or 10s. per bottle.—Of the red wines that are grown in the vicinity of Bordeaux, namely the claret, Mr. Henderson remarks that "the Lafitte, Latour, Chateau-Margaux, and Haut-Brion, are so greatly esteemed, that they always sell from 20 to 25 per cent. higher than any others of the prov. The first mentioned is the most choice and delicate, and is characterized by its silky softness on the palate, and its charming perfume, which partakes of the nature of the violet and raspberry. The Latour has a fuller body, and at the same time a considerable aroma, but wants the softness of the Lafitte. The Chateau-Margaux, on the other hand, is lighter, and possesses all the delicate qualities of the Lafitte, except that it has not quite so high a flavour. The Haut-Brion has more spirit and body than any of the preceding, but is rough when new, and requires to be kept six or seven years in the wood; while the others become fit for bottling in much less time. In favourable years, the produce of Lafitte, Latour, and Chateau- Margaux, sells at from 3,000 to 3,300 francs the tun, which contains 242 gallons; and when they have been kept in the chais or vault for six years, the price is doubled; so that, even at Bordeaux, a bottle of the best wine cannot be purchased at less than 6 francs." For more than 14 centuries the wine most approved of in France was from the environs of Paris. The wines of Surenne, Ruel, and Montmorency, which are now considered the most common, and sold at the cheapest rate, were then served at the tables of the rich as a luxury. The brandies made in F. are esteemed the best in the world, and are distilled in every part of the kingdom where vines are grown; in the distillation, not only wines of an inferior, but also those of the best quality are used. The brandies of Nantes, Cogniac, and Poitou, are the most esteemed of all the French brandies, as they have a finer taste, and are stronger. The English, Dutch, Flemish, and 100,000 livres: the profit being part of the revenue Hamburghers, take off the greatest part of these brandies. Vinegar is made in Provence, Guyenne, Orleannais, Anjou, Aunis, Brittany, &c.; that of Orleans is esteemed the best. Cyder is made in most of those provinces whose climate is unfavourable to the grape. Normandy is particularly famous for this liquor, where they also make brandy of it. Beetroot sugar.] From the progress making in F. in the manufacture of beetroot sugar, it appears that more importance is at present attached to that branch of commercial industry among our Gallic neighbours than during the period when Napoleon's continental system induced them to turn their thoughts to this substitute for the colonial article. The expensive process for the manufacture of beetroot sugar has till now been the cause which has kept up its price on a par with the sugar of West India growth; and, to diminish this, so far as finally to make the former more marketable, so that F. may one day dispense with the foreign supply of an article of so paramount a necessity in that country, seems to engross the attention of landowners, growers, refiners, and chemists. The annual consumption of this sugar now amounts to between 7,000,000 and 8,000,000 lbs. It appears that the beetroot sugar factories in work in 1844-5 were 294; manufactories not in work were 21. The quantity of sugar produced was 32,373,449 kilogrammes. The quantity of sugar manufactured during the year 1846 amounted to 39,403,754 kilogrammes (nearly 40,000 tons); the amount sold for consumption, 35,690,608; and the duties levied thereon, 9,187,615 f. (£360,000). As compared with the results of 1845, there was an increase in the production of 3,863,504 kilogrammes (3,863 tons); in the consumption, of 4,212,627; and in the duties, of 3,142,457 f. The number of factories at work in 1846 was 306. Woodlands.] The woods and forests of F. are numerous and extensive, occupying about one-eighth of the surface; and, as wood is still the general fuel, attention to their culture is indispensable. A committee appointed by the first national assembly to inquire into the extent of the forests, reported that the whole extent of surface covered with wood amounted to 13,100,691 arpens, of 100 perches of 28 sq. French feet each; or 10,143,000 English acres. But, according to M. Neckar and Mr. Young, the woods of F. amounted to 22,289,016 arpens; Chaptal assigns them an extent of 7,072,000 hectares, or 17,476,114 acres; and, if we estimate at the annual value of 10s. per acre, the sum total of the annual value of the woods will be above £8,000,000 sterling. One of the Paris journals, in alluding to the question of replanting the hills near the Loire with timber trees, in 1846, gives the following account of the present quantity of timber-land in F.: - Of the 52,000,000 of hectares in France, 8,623,128 are in woods and forests; of these, 1,073,256 are the property of the state; 1,823,833 belong to the communes or public establishments; 106,929 to the Crown, and 5,619,110 to private persons. It is added that the forests of the state are the best attended to, and therefore the most productive; they yield a revenue of 32 fr. per hectare, whilst the woods and forests of the communes and private persons only yield 24 fr.—The forests of Orleans, the Ardennes, and Fountainbleau, are the most extensive. The forest of Orleans lies to the N of that city and of the river Loire. It covers a surface of more than 100,000 acres; and contains several plains and villages. It is 15 leagues in length; but of very unequal breadth, being in some places 7 or 8 leagues, and in others only 2 or 3 broad. Before the Revolution the value of timber annually felled in this forest amounted to of the duke of Orleans. Numerous banditti formerly haunted it; and it is still infested by immense numbers of wolves. The forest of Ardennes was in Cæsar's time the largest in Gaul; and extended from the conflux of the Rhine and Moselle on the E, as far as Artois on the W,-a space of 240 m.; and from the S frontier of Luxemburg to the Waal on the N,—or 150 m. in breadth. This forest has been cleared in a great many places, especially towards its extremities. It, however, still extends over the greater part of the duchy of Luxemburg, the S part of the bishoprie of Liege, and the northern part of Champagne. Here, during the middle ages, the sons of chivalry, in the true spirit of their order, roamed in quest of adventure, and here were performed some of their most splendid achievements.—The forest of Fountainbleau, anciently called the forest of Biere, covers 26,424 acres of ground, including many empty places where the timber has been cut down. Under the old regime, the national forests embraced 3,000,000 arpens; and yielded 12,000,000 francs to the royal treasury. At the Revolution, all the forests held by the corporate bodies and the emigrants were annexed to those of the state; which were thus increased to upwards of 4,000,000 arpens. These, added to the Belgian forests, and those on the W bank of the Rhine, in the year 1806 yielded rather more than 70,000,000 of francs. All forests of more than 300 acres were also added to the national domains, and declared unalienable. Silk.] Dr. Bowring, in his Second Report on the Commercial relations between F. and Great Britain, has made some curious calculations of the quantities of the silk annually obtained, the worms, mulberrytrees, &c., in the former country. He says, "It is pretty clearly ascertained that the present production [1834] of raw silk in F. is about 3,000,000 lbs. per annum; the quantity of cocoons may therefore be estimated at about 36,000,000 lbs. It is calculated that 16 lbs. of mulberry leaves will be consumed for the production of a pound of cocoons; so that the annual produce of F. must be 576,000,000 lbs. of leaves; and giving to each tree an average annual produce of 80 lbs., the number of mulberry trees must be above 7,000,000. One ounce of eggs will, on an average, produce 100 lbs. of cocoons. An ounce of eggs is calculated to give at least 30,000 worms; the quantity of silkworms annually reared in F. cannot, therefore, be less than 10,800,000,000. The general estimate of the manufacturers is, that the consumption is about one-fourth of foreign and three-fourths of French silk. The looms of F. are estimated at about 70,000; making allowance, therefore, for those which employ other raw materials besides silk, the average quantity of raw silk wrought up in France is believed to be about 60 lbs. per loom, which gives an annual consumption of 4,200,000 lbs. It is generally estimated that the present value of the silk manufactures of F. is about 140,000,000 francs [£5,600,000], composed of 80,000,000 frs. value of raw materials, and 60,000,000 frs. of added value by labour, profit, &c. As 20 francs [16s.] per lb. may be deemed a fair average value for silk, it would appear that about 4,000,000 lbs. must be the weight annually consumed." Horses.] The horses of France have never been celebrated either for size, swiftness, or beauty; her ancient monarchs were drawn to the national assemblies by oxen. Previous to the first Revolution, coach and saddle-horses formed no small part of British commerce with F. But Normandy has been long noted as producing the best horses in F.; and William the Conqueror, it is affirmed, won the decisive battle of Hastings by the superiority of his cavalry, which were all of Norman breed. The Norman horses are generally low and thick, very steady, sure, and strong. The Limousin horses have been improved by crossing them with the Turkish, Arabian, and English breeds. Auvergne produces good hacks. A great number of horses are also bred in Franche-Comte, especially in the hilly part of the country. Colts reared in the latter district are bought, when six months or a year old, by the horse dealers in Campagne, Burgundy, Brie, and Berry. Upon the whole, the quality of the breed, and probably the number of horses, has declined since the Revolution. Napoleon—who owed many of his victories to his immense superiority in cavalry—paid particular attention to the breed and supply of horses; but the Russian campaign and the disasters which succeeded, so much thinned their numbers, that in 1819 the horses and mules in France amounted together to only 1,657,571. General Oudinot recommends that the French government should extend its breeding establishments so as to be independent of a supply from abroad; and also of private breeders in F., who are unable, he says, to provide the number of good horses required for the use of the cavalry. In reply to General Oudinot, M. de Torcy, an agriculturist, states that, if the government finds a difficulty in obtaining horses from the agriculturists, it is because there is no system in the mode of remounting the cavalry, the number of horses required in some years being so small that no person thinks it worth his while to turn his attention to breeding for the use of the army, and the price offered being unremunerative. In 1839 (says this gentleman) the regulation price was only 390 fr.; in 1835, 430 fr.; and in 1842, 480 fr.; and the price now proposed is 500 fr., which he contends is much too low to give encouragement to breeders. As regards the supposed difficulty of procuring the number of horses in F. which would be wanted annually, if the mode of supply were to be regular, M. de Torcy declares that the resources of French agriculturists are sufficient, as 187,000 colts are produced in F. annually, of which 9,000 to 10,000 are fit for the cavalry. The number of horses in Paris is singularly small; indeed the total number of horses kept for amusement in France does not exceed 8,000! The £17 a-head. Mules are much employed in the middle and south of France for treading out the corn. In the dep. of Aveyron, especially near Rhodez, a vast number of mules are fed; and in the two yearly fairs at Rhodez, the trade produces about 300,000 crowns. The mules of Poitou are particularly celebrated for their size and beauty. Cattle.] More than half the tillage of F. is performed by oxen, and in the mountainous districts small cows are generally employed. Perche. Champagne, Lorraine, Alsace, Flanders, Normandy, Brittany, Le Maine, Anjou, Poitou, Berry, Nivernais, Burgundy, Limousin, Auvergne, Bresse, Languedoc, and Dauphine, are the provs. where oxen and cows are principally bred and fattened. The prevalent colour of the cattle from Paris to the Pyrenees, is a pale reddish, or rather a cream colour. The cattle of the Limousin are the best in F., and decidedly of this colour. They are short in the legs, have straight flat backs, well-arched ribs, deep and heavy carcasses, and their weight is from 60 to 80 stones of 14 lb. In a little island formed by the two mouths of the Rhone, called Camargue, which forms an equilateral triangle of 7 leagues, and was formerly covered with wood, but is now cleared and clothed with verdant pastures, vast numbers of sheep and oxen are bred and fattened; and 3,000 horses are also computed to be bred annually here. The oxen are reserved for the supply of the marine at Toulon, and are generally a small black Hungarian breed, similar to our Scottish cattle. The Norman cows give the best and the most milk. Those of Lower Normandy, Brittany, and the Boulonnais, supply excellent butter. Gournay, in Lower Normandy, is particularly noted for its market of fine fresh butter, from which Paris is chiefly supplied. As cows are rare at Marseilles, milk is furnished from sheep and goats. Cheese is very little made in France. Normandy, Languedoc, Provence, Brittany, Forez, and Brie, furnish it in the greatest quantity; that of Brie is esteemed the best. F. on the whole is very inadequately stocked with horned cattle, and is obliged to supply this deficiency by extensive importation. The cattle of F. are estimated at 7,000,000, being, upon equal surfaces, about half the stock of England; the supply of beef, milk, butter, and cheese for each inhabitant of France, is but one-third of the quantity consumed by each individual in England. Sheep.] There are five native breeds of sheep in F.; viz.—1st, The Picardy, hornless, white-faced, with silky hanging ears; their wool coarse and of middling length. They are probably a bastard Flemish breed. 2d, The Norman, red-legged and faced; wool coarse. 3d, The Berry breed, somewhat resembling the South-down sheep; the wool fine. 4th, The Roussillon, resembling the Spanish sheep, with very fine wool. 5th, There is a kind of sheep, near Mirepoix, resembling the Norfolk breed, with horns, black legs and faces. The peculiarities of French sheep are their long legs, thin carcasses, and coarse wool; and the mutton is generally bad. The sheep of the N are however larger, and yield a much finer fleece than those of the S. Merino sheep were introduced into France by Louis in 1786. There were four establishments of Merino sheep: viz. Rambouillet, Alfert, Perpignan, and Pompadour; but the demand for their wool was still further depressed by the Revolution. Buonaparte, in 1811, published a decree, by which he expected to cover France with fine-woolled flocks; but laws seldom meddle with private interests without proving detrimental. In the exposé for 1814, Buonaparte's forced attempts to introduce the Merinos are asserted to have cost the government 200,000,000 francs; and after all, so price of farm-horses in the south of France averages | far from having succeeded in his object, the breeds of native sheep have been rather deteriorated. Sheep | are kept in all parts of France, but principally in Roussillon, Languedoc, Provence, Dauphiné, Auvergne, Guyenne, Gascony, Bearn, Limousin, Marche, Poitou, Mayenne, Anjou, Brittany, Touraine, Champagne, Alsace, Franche - Comté, Normandy, and French Flanders. Sheep, in most parts of F., are shut up in stables at night, and sheltered from the sun at noon during summer. They are generally folded in the field till November. When the snow is deep, they are sometimes fed on the branches of trees. On the Cevennes, which run along the N side of Eastern Languedoc, numerous flocks are fed in summer upon the aromatic herbs with which these mountains abound. They descend into the plains during the winter cold. But the most extensive and singular emigration of sheep is that which takes place annually, and as regularly as in Spain, from the Camargue, or Delta of the Rhone, and the stony desert of La Crau, to the mountains of Provence and Dauphiné, especially to those of Gap and Barcellonetta, and back again. The migration to the mountains takes place in May; and they return in October or November, and sometimes sooner. It is conducted with all the regularity and order of an army on a march. The flocks belong to several proprietors, who principally reside near the mountains of the Rhone. The wages of the shepherds are generally high; and they are in every respect a superior class of men to those in our country. The wages of a chief shepherd are about £12 sterling. Besides this, he is allowed a certain sum for every sheep sold, his board at 11 franc a-day, and a free cottage for his family. The wages of an inferior shepherd are about £8 sterling; with the same allowance for board as the chief shepherd. The average weight of the native fleeces is about 2½ or 3 lbs.; that of the Merinos 6 lbs. The wool of the former is generally but of an indifferent quality. The wool of Roussillon is the finest; that of Narbonne is nearly equal, but more cottony, and of a shorter fibre. The wool of Beziers is next in quality; that of Languedoc is somewhat less fine. The wool produced on the seacoast is coarse and heavy. The wools of the mountains of Montpellier and De Sommieres are of three sorts; the first is equal to the wool of Pesenas; the second less fine; and the third very coarse. wool of Berry is fine; that of Rheims inferior. The number of sheep in France was estimated in 1819 at 30,307,728; and the total of the wool they yielded at 106,770,000 lbs. At present they amount to nearly 36,000,000. - The Pyrenean breed of shepherd-dogs are particularly celebrated. They are black and white, equal in size to a large wolf, have a large head and neck, and are armed with collars stuck full of iron spikes, so that no wolf can attack them. Goats and Poultry.] A great number of goats are kept in F., principally of course in the mountainous parts. In 1819, M. Ternaux imported some of the celebrated goats of Thibet into F. These animals, it is said, have become perfectly habituated to the climate, and bear equally well the keen air of the Pyrenees, and the heat of the central plains. The Cashmere shawls manufactured by M. Ternaux from the fine silky hair or duvet of the Thibetian goat have already acquired a high reputation. Pigs are chiefly fed in the neighbourhood of woods, or where grain is plenty. They are also fed on acorns; and in the Limousin on chestnuts. Poultry constitutes one of the principal articles in the rural economy of France; there is perhaps a greater weight consumed of it than of mutton. Eggs are an important article of export. The quantity of French eggs imported into Britain in 1827 was 63,000,000; it now exceeds 150,000,000. Manufactures.] Although F. has always been most distinguished by the indigenous productions of her soil, yet she early attained such eminence in manufactures and commerce as to become not only the rival of Britain, but even for some time to enjoy the superiority. Her natural advantages for commerce are great; but, owing to many concurring causes, her manufactures and commerce have not kept pace with those of this country.—The earliest notice that we have of the manufactures of F. occurs in St. Jerome, who speaks of a manufacture of stuffs at Arras, much esteemed in his time, which was the 4th cent. The woollen manufactures of F. were extensive and important in the beginning of the 14th cent. The first regular attempt to establish the silk-manufacture was made by Francis I., who used great efforts to procure workmen from Milan; but the French soon made great progress in this manufacture, principally at Lyons and other parts of the S of France, whence many parts of Europe were and still are supplied with silk goods. At Troyes leather and parchment were first prepared with some degree of dexterity: the people of that place were likewise early celebrated for the goodness of their dyes. The French were at this period very deficient in the manufacture of iron and steel, and procured their fire-arms from Lombardy. Charles IX. endeavoured to introduce musquets made at Metz and Abbeville, but they were so heavily and awkwardly made that the attempt was abandoned. Italian manufactures were brought by the same monarch to Paris; where the armourers finished the head-pieces made and purchased at Milan. Gunpowder was made in F. during the reign of Henry III.; but not in such quantities as to render the importation of it unnecessary; Genoa, in particular, supplied the French with gunpowder. The reign of Henry IV., and the administration of the great Sully, must be regarded as the first grand era of the manufactures of F. Sully, more friendly to agriculture than to commerce or manufactures, could not indeed fully enter into the views of his master, yet undoubtedly the monarch profited by the penetration and good sense of the minister. Henry erected temporary buildings at Fountainbleau, the castle of Madrid, and the Tuilleries, for rearing silk-worms; and caused mulberry-trees to be planted in various provs. where they had not been previously cultivated; but the attempt to rear the silk-worm as far N as Paris proved abortive. The good effects of Henry's exertions and perseverance, however, appeared before his death. Lyons soon attracted the notice of foreigners, and was speedily enriched by its silk manufactures; so much so that in seven years from its establishment, the southern provs. of Dauphine, Languedoc, and Provence, are said to have derived from it greater annual profits than from the joint produce of their oil, wines, and sweet-meats, the natural and ancient products of the country. But the silk manufacture was not the only object of Henry's attention. Gold and silver tissues of various kinds, and of exquisite beauty, were manufactured at Paris, by Milanese weavers, whom Henry induced to settle under his immediate protection in the capital. The Gobelins' tapestry, of such unequalled delicacy, and so much admired over all Europe, was begun in one of the suburbs of Paris, under the direction of Flemish artisans. The manufacture of mirrors in imitation of those cast at Venice, which had been formerly attempted under Henry III., was again successfully revived at Paris and Nevers; and earthen-ware equalling the Italian in beauty was fabricated. Crapes equal to those of Bologna were manufactured in the castle of Mantes-sur-Seine; and the manufacture of l linen was begun. Before this reign, iron had been cut and split by the hand; but mills for those purposes were now established on the river Estampes; and steel, previously imported from Piedmont, began to be manufactured in the suburb of St. Victor in Paris. Manufactures of gauzes and linens, as well as of woollen cloth and serges, were also encouraged and extended by Henry. The second grand era of French manufacture and commerce, was the age of Louis XIV. It was that monarch's felicity to possess and enjoy the matured talents of the great Colbert, under whose administration F. attained a height of military fame and grandeur unknown in former periods of her history. The principal new manufactures that were either introduced, or established and extended in this era, were those of Sedan, Abbeville, the Gobelins, and the glass-manufacture of St. Gobin. The manufacture of fine cloth at Sedan owed its origin and perfection to Nicolas Cadeau who had obtained a knowledge of the mode of manufacture practised in Holland. Entering into partnership with two rich Parisian merchants in 1646, they obtained a patent for the manufacture of woollen cloths made after the fabric and manner of Dutch cloths. When the exclusive monopoly of Cadean and his partners was on the point of expiring, a proposal for commencing another manufacture of fine cloths at Abbeville, in imitation of those of Spain and Holland, was made by Vanrobais, a Dutch merchant. Colbert instantly acceded to the proposal, and Vanrobais being brought from Holland, was settled with his workmen at Abbeville. Thirty woollen looms were set on foot, with as many fulling-mills as were deemed necessary. In 1708, the looms exceeded 100; and about 600 persons, men, women, and children, were employed in picking the wool, and winding, warping, weaving, and shearing the cloth.
