By a fourth patent, noblemen were permitted to enter into partnership with cloth-weavers, without derogation to their titles or honour. The manufacture of tapestry established by Henry IV. was revived with more success by Colbert; and obtained the name of Gobelines, from the circumstance of the house in which the manufacture was carried on having been built by two brothers of that name, who first brought to Paris the secret of the scarlet dye which also preserves their name. Colbert had rebuilt and ornamented the royal palaces, particularly the Louvre and the Tuilleries, and was anxious that they should be splendidly furnished. With this view he collected together some of the most able workmen in F., especially painters, tapestry-weavers, goldsmiths, and workers in ebony. The tapestry-weavers were procured from Flanders; separate superintendents of the raised and of the smooth tapestries were appointed; and another Fleming was vested with the management of the wool-dyeing department. The manufacture of tapestry commenced in 1663, but did not flourish till 1666, when it was endowed with many privileges. The celebrated painter Le Brun was appointed chief director of the Gobelin manufactures; to which he communicated that beauty and grandeur which his admirable talents were so well calculated to introduce. The glass-manufactures introduced by Henry II. and Henry IV. were brought to perfection under Louis XIV.; and the glass of St. Gobin was deemed equal to that of Venice both in quality and quantity. The progress of this manufacture was retarded at first from a scarcity of wood. The manufactory was therefore removed into the vicinity of an extensive forest, with the advantage of a river to save landcarriage. The whole is situated at the top of a small hill close to the village of St. Gobin, near La Fere, in Picardy. The very white sand used in the manufacture is brought from the neighbourhood of Creil, a place 11 leagues distant from Paris. The glasses are sent by water to that cap., where they are polished and silvered. In 1685, different immunities were granted to Noel de Varennes, in order to encourage him to carry on in Languedoc a manufactory of cloth similar to that which was sent from London to Turkey. Louis appropriated the sum of 1,000,000 livres yearly, exclusive of all immunities from customs, to engage and reward skilful masters and artificers, who undertook to set up fabrics of cloth, silk, camel and goats' hair, crystal, and other commodities. In 1695, the tin-manufacture was organized in various parts of F., by Isaac Robelin, who obtained an exclusive patent for this purpose; and in 1703, the manufacture of serges and baizes, which had been established after the union of the crowns of France and Spain, rivalled those of England. The unjust and impolitic revocation of the edict of Nantes gave a fatal blow to some of the most flourishing and lucrative manufactures of F. The people driven out of the kingdom by that illadvised measure were generally the best merchants, artificers, and manufacturers in the country. Those who had money retired into England and Holland; but the more industrious part of them settled in Brandenburg, where they introduced the manufacture of serge, cloths, druggets, crapes, caps, stockings, and hats, and also the dyeing of all sorts of colours. The goldsmiths, jewellers, watch-makers, and carvers amongst them principally settled in Britain; and to them England is indebted for her silk-manufactures, and also for various improvements in manufacturing paper, hats, glass, watches, cutlery, and hardware. It is impossible to fix with precision the first introduction of the cotton-manufacture into F. It was carried on in Normandy previous to the middle of the 18th cent. Before 1747, the manufacture of cottons-or 'cotton-linens' as they were then called—was established at Nantes in Bretagne. The most flourishing period of the French manufactures was from 1650 to 1750; subsequently to the last mentioned period, several causes proved extremely unfavourable to their rapid development and increase. Woollen manufactures.] We have seen that the fabrication of woollen goods was early introduced into F. It is only, however, of late years that the introduction of carding-machines, and other ameliorations in the system of spinning and weaving, has placed woollen cloths at the command of the pop. in general. In 1812 the quantity of native wool manufactured in F. was 35,000,000 of kilogrammes; in 1826 it was 42,000,000 kilogs. of native wool, and 4,500,000 of foreign wool; in 1836 about 50,000,000 kilogs. of native wool were consumed in F. Much has been done to ameliorate the quality of the native fleeces; and the best superfine blue cloths now sell at Louviers for 40 f. the ell, very good being to be had at 20 f.; good broad-cloths are also made at Elbœuf, and Vire, in Normandy. The towns in the centre and S of F. are also beginning to compete with the fabrics of the N; the flannels of Rheims, from their cheapness and durability, form an excellent article of clothing for the poor. The carpets of the Gobelins and the Savonnerie, from the length of time required to perfect them, are seldom valued at less than £200 or £300 each; the next class are those of Aubusson and Felletin. The exportation of French cloth is diminishing in proportion to the decrease in the stock of foreign wool. Silk manufactures.] The manufacture for which F. is eminently distinguished is that of silk. The deps. of Herault, Ardèche, Gard, Vaucluse, Lozere, and Drome, all in the S of F., are those in which chiefly raw silk is produced. Raw silk was imported for a long time into F. after the introduction of the ! manufacture. Its main seat has always been at Lyons, where the rich and brilliant stuffs used chiefly for furniture are made, and in the neighbourhood of which also the ribbon-trade is carried on; but the great mart of exhibition and sale is the metropolis. The other seats of this important manufacture are Nismes, Avignon, St. Etienne, Alais, Tours, and Paris. Before the revocation of the edict of Nantes, from 1650 to 1680, the number of silk-looms at Lyons varied from 9,000 to 12,000. From 1689 to 1699, a few years after the revocation, they were reduced to 4,000. In 1750, under a more tolerant regime, the number increased to 12,000. From 1780 to 1788, it was 18,000. The revolution brought down the number to 3,500, and under the Empire the number was 12,000. The re-establishment of peace brought the number to 27,000 in 1827, 40,000 in 1835, and 50,000 in 1844. Since then the number has kept up, notwithstanding the late revolution, with this difference, that the town of Lyons has lost a little, whilst the country-districts have gained. Independently of the circle of Lyons, there may be reckoned at Nismes, Avignon, Paris, in Picardy, Lorraine, and Flanders, about 20,000 looms for silk stuffs and silk lace, 15,000 for various other kinds of silks, and 20.000 for ribands, giving a total of upwards of 100,000 looms. Estimating at 30 kilogs. the silk per annum for each loom, and at 3,000 f. the average obtained from its produce, we shall have an approximate sum of 300,000,000 f. for the silk manufacture in F., one-half of which is disposed of abroad, and the rest at home; and more than onehalf of the raw material consumed is of native production. The china-worm has been naturalized in F., especially in the deps. of Gard, Drome, Vaucluse, and Ardeche; and the white French crapes now rival those of the East, so long unattainable, in brilliance and purity. Since the peace, red and blue embroidered stuffs have been largely exported from F. to Persia and Turkey. Cotton manufactures.] Prior to 1786 Swiss cottons were generally used in F. About 1789 the average value of English cottons imported into F., at a duty we believe of 10 per cent., was about 25,000,000 f., or rather more than £1,000,000, a large proportion of which was of the finer fabrics. Napoleon's 'Continental system' made it a matter of necessity for the French to resort to their own resources; and they so far succeeded, that the quantity of cotton spun in 1812 was equal to the average of that spun in England in the ten years ending with 1797: thus bringing them to a station as important as that occupied by us 15 years before. Since that period, cotton-factories have spread widely through Picardy and Normandy. From 1827 to 1836, the average value of the year's importation of cotton was 71,000,000 francs. In 1839, the quantity of cotton imported amounted in value to 82,000,000 francs; in 1840, to 106,000,000 f.; and in 1841, to 112,000,000 f.; in 1844, the value was 111,000,000 f., of which 105,000,000 f. represented the amount worked up within F. Rouen is the Manchester of France, and its proximity to Havre-de-Grace, the great American port, gives it the same advantage in point of situation as Manchester derives from being near Liverpool. It is computed that the cotton-trade of Rouen supports from 55,000 to 60,000 persons. The corduroy and velveteen of Troyes are well-suited for labourers' clothing; Caen produces cambric-muslins equal to those of Switzerland; the ginghams from the Upper Rhine are stout and well-dyed, and the printed muslins of Versailles rival those of England. The French cambrics have long been unrivalled. French laces are made both of silk and thread. The point-lace of Alençon has long enjoyed a great name throughout France, England, and Germany; also that of Argentan. Miscellaneous manufactures.] Clocks and watches are made in different quarters, but chiefly at Paris. The French are clever in the construction of surgical and mathematical instruments: in the capital, every article of this kind, as well as all sorts of expensive and tasteful toys, are carried to great perfection. China is principally manufactured at Sevres, and enjoys a high degree of reputation. The glassmanufacture of St. Gobin still retains its pre-eminence for large and beautiful mirrors. The crystalmanufactories in and near Paris in particular are very numerous. The glass-manufactures of F. produced in 1842, 32,711,545 f., and gave employment to 13,797 individuals. French furniture is in general cheaper than ours. Dyeing is carried on to a great extent in the vicinity of the silk, woollen, linen, and cotton-manufactories. The waters of the Saone are excellent for scouring, possessing, it is said, a soapy quality; those of the Rhone, being pure, are equally fitted for dyeing. The waters of the little river Sornin are also reckoned remarkably good for dyeing; indeed, generally speaking, the superior excellence of the French cloths is supposed to result, in regard to colour, from the great purity of the waters used in dyeing. Except in articles of silk and of iron, the great manufactures of F, are, as in England, mainly conducted in the northern and central parts of the country. Thus Rheims, Amiens, Sedan, Louviers, Elbœuf, Beauvais, Vire, Tours, and Abbeville, all towns in Normandy, Picardy, French Flanders, and adjacent provs., are the places in which woollens are mostly made; though some are manufactured at Castres, Aubusson, Carcassonne, Vienne, and other towns in the S. Rouen, Lille, Roubaix, Turcoing, St. Quentin, Mulhausen, &c., besides Valenciennes, Chantilly, and Bayeux, for lace, are the principal seats of the cotton manufactures; and many of them, together with Laval, Rennes, and other towns in Brittany, Cambray, Donai, &c., the places whence the linen goods chiefly come. Paper, pasteboard. and paper-hangings, indeed-important articles of produce, since their annual value is as much as 43,000,000 f.—are made principally at Annonay, Angoulême, Vienne, Montauban, Nismes, and other places in the S and W; as are kid-gloves, morocco leather, watch machinery, wedgewood and other earthenware, mirrors, &c. But beet-root sugar, which is produced to an amount yearly averaging £4,480,000 in value, comes wholly from the N deps., and eminently from that entitled 'the North,' in which the towns of Dunkerque, Lille, and Valenciennes are situated. Sevres, which has an European fame for its porcelain, is in what is officially regarded as one of the N deps.; and Paris, in the adjacent dep., almost monopolizes the manufactures of gold and silver lace, bronze and some other metallic wares, joiners' goods, several chemical products, tapestry, artificial flowers, watches, and articles of taste and virtu, which come under the indefinite name of 'French goods.' Steam-power.] At the end of 1841 there were in France 179 establishments having steam-power, containing 5,600 boilers, of which 1,889 were for the purposes of heating, and 3,511 for giving motion to machinery. There were, besides, 2,637 engines, the aggregate of which was equal to 39,779 horses' power. At the same period there were 260 steam-boats, being 128 more than in 1838, without comprising those belonging to the state. The number of engines in these steam-vessels was 400, of a force equalling together 45,000 horses' power. The number of passengers conveyed by these vessels was 2,500,000, being 800,000 more than in the preceding year; and the increase on freight was equally remarkable, having exceeded by more than 60,000 tons the quantity thus conveyed in 1840. The locomotives employed on the railroads in the deps. of the Seine, Rhone, Herault, and Loire, were in number 118, and in force upwards of 300 horses' power. Of these, about 35 were of French manufacture.-The statistical returns for 1849, relative to the steam-engines and apparatus at present working in F. in all the manufactories, and in the trading-vessels navigating rivers or seas, present the following results:- There now exist in F. 5,607 manufactories of various denominations in which steam-machinery is employed. This machinery is worked by means of boilers, the number of which is 9,288, and 8,776 of which were made in French establishments. These boilers represent a force of 65,120 horses' power, calculating the horse-power as 75 kilogrammes (180 lbs.) raised one metre (1 yard) per second These boilers represent the force that would be produced by 195,361 draught-houses and 1,367,530 labourers. The length of railway open for traffic was 2,171 kilometres (1,300 m.), on which were employed 725 locomotives. The number of steam trading-vessels was 279; their tonnage 40,098 tons. They were propelled by 502 engines, constituting a total power of 22,893 horses. The merchandise transported by these vessels amounted to 730,948 tons. The progress of steamnavigation in F. is indicated in the following facts:-In 1835 there were 75 steamers, by which 1,038,916 passengers were carried, and 88,140 tons of merchandise; in 1840, there were 211 steamers, 2,547,116 passengers, and 485,539 tons; in 1845, there were 259 steamers, 3,461,336 passengers, and 696,066 tons; and in 1849, 279 steamers, 2,808,886 passengers, and 730,948 tons. The total number of steam-engines employed by land or sea in F., it would appear from the above statements, amount to 6,432: and constitute together a force of 110,178 steam-horses, or 330,535 draught-horses. External commerce.] Colbert reckoned that, in 1669, F. employed only 600 ships in foreign commerce. At the first revolution, the number of ships employed in long voyages amounted to about 1,000, averaging 250 tons each; and the exports to different European states employed 580,000 tons of shipping, of which, however, little more than one-fourth were French. In 1825, the whole of the tonnage employed in the foreign commerce of F. did not amount to a half of that employed by the United States; and to little more than a fourth of that employed by this country; and while about one-half of the commerce of F. was transacted by foreigners, only one-third of that of Great Britain, and one-tenth of that of the United States, was engrossed by foreign bottoms. It appears from a report by the minister of commerce, on the situation of the foreign trade of F. between 1787 and 1810, and 1825 and 1836, that, during the latter period, the increase of the commercial transactions of F. amounted in Europe to 361,000,000 francs, or nearly one-half; in Africa, to 18,000,000 f., or two-thirds; in Asia, to 39,000,000 f., or more than seven times their amount; in America, to 225,000,000 f., or nearly double; and finally, in the colonies and fisheries, to 22,000,000 f., or one-fifth. On the whole, there was an increase in the general commerce of F., during those ten years, of one-half, or 668,000,000 f. In 1825 its amount was 1,200,000,000 f.; in 1836 it had reached 1,866,000,000 f. The total external commerce of F. in 1840 amounted to 2,063,000,000 f., being 697,000,000 f. more than the decennial average from 1827 to 1836. Of the two items which constituted this total in 1840, viz., the importations and the exportations - and between which the general amount is pretty equally divided—the first was that in which the increase chiefly occurred. At no former period did the value of the imports ever amount to a milliard; but they now exceeded the latter sum by 52,000,000 f. The general export commerce in 1840 (1,011,000,000 f.) only exceeded by about 1 per cent. the extent of the same commerce during 1839; but on comparing it the 5 preceding years, an increase of 12 per cent. was shown. The special commerce,—that is to say, the commerce which includes on the one hand the foreign productions which F. imports for her own consumption, and on the other those arising from her own soil or her manufacturing industry which she sends abroad,-included in 1840, in the sum total of 2,063,000,000, a value of 1,442,000,000 f.: viz., importations 747,000,000 f., and exports 695,000,000 f. The increase of the special commerce equally occurred in the value of the imports and in that of the exports, especially in the former. A comparison with the year 1839, and the average of the preceding 5 years, showed an increase in favour of 1840, viz., of 15 and 26 per cent. in the importations, and of 3 and 14 per cent. in the exportations. Considered in relation to the distinction existing between the sea and land-commerce, the total amount of the commercial investment in 1840 was divided as follows: Sea-commerce, 1,481,000,000, equal to 71.8 per cent. Land-commerce, 582,000,000, equal to 28°2 per cent. The value of the articles of which the land-commerce and the united imports and exports consisted, was distributed between the various countries of importation and exportation in the following proportions: Switzerland, 161,000,000 or 27 per cent. . 125,000,000 or 22 Belgium, . . ... Sardinian states, . . . Total, The value of the entire sea-commerce was distributed in 1840 amongst the countries of Europe, those not in Europe, the French colonies, and the great fisheries, in the following manner: Countries in Europe, . 757,000,000 or 51 per cent. Countries out of Europe, 582,000,000 or 39 Colonies and fisheries, . 142,000,000 or 10 ... 1,481,000,000 or 100 Total, . *** The general commerce of F. during the year 1844 amounted to 2,340,000,000 francs, exceeding that of 1843 by 161,000,000 f., or 7 per cent., and exceeding the average of the 5 preceding years by 248,000,000 f., or 12 per cent. This increase was much more on exportation than on importation. The foreign produce entered for French consumption amounted to 867,000,000 f., showing an increase of 3 and 11 per cent. on a comparison with 1843 and the 5 years' average. The French produce exported abroad amounted to 790,000,000 f., presenting on the two comparisons an increase of 15 and 14 per cent.—Considering the trade as carried on by sea and land, it was found that the commerce by sea amounted to 1,658,000,000 f., and that by land to 682,000,000 f.; that is to say, the sea-commerce was more than twothirds of the whole. Compared with 1843 and the 5 years' average, this was an increase of 6 and 10 per cent. for the sea-commerce, and 12 and 17 for the land. Of the total amount of goods carried by sea (1,658,000,000 f.), as much as 764,000,000 f. was carried in French bottoms, and 894,000,000 f. by foreigners. This gave an increase of 6 and 11 per cent. under French, and 6 and 9 per cent. under foreign flags. The countries with which there was the most trade in 1844 were the United States, England, Switzerland, Sardinian states, German Zollverein, Spain, Algeria, and the colonies of the Antilles and Bourbon. These countries are set down for 72 per cent. of the total amount of imports and exports combined. In 1843, the proportion of these countries was only 69 per cent. The total aggregate value of the imports into, and with the average resulting from the combination of the exports from F. during 1848, amounted, according to the official rate of valuation fixed in 1826, to 2,015,000,000 f.; showing a decrease of 599,000,000 f., or 23 per cent., as compared with 1847. When compared with the average of the 5 previous years, the decrease in 1848 was 384,000,000 f., or 16 per cent. Calculated according to the new tariff valuation issued in 1849, but which had not then come into operation, the total value of the French commerce was reduced to 1,645,000,000 f. A diminution of 370,000,000 f., or 18 per cent., is thus apparent in the gross official returns of the commerce of 1848. For the sake, however, of perspicuity, the tariff rate of valuation fixed in 1826 is adhered to in the following analysis. Of this amount of 2,015,000,000 f., as given officially, the imports were 862,000,000 f., and the exports 1,153,000,000 f. As compared with 1847, the value of the products imported shows a decrease of 481,000,000 f., or 36 per cent.; on a comparison with the average 5 years the falling-off is 382,000,000 f., or 31 per cent. The decrease in the exports is 118,000,000 f., or 9 per cent. as compared with 1847, and only 2,000,000 f. as compared with the average of 5 years. -As regards the mode of conveyance by which these 2,015,000,000 f. worth of produce entered and left the French territory, we find that the maritime commerce represented a value of 1,441,000,000 f. official value, or 1,176,000,000 f. actual value; whilst the trade by land was 574,000,000 f. official value, or 469,000,000 f. actual value. The proportion between the two modes of transport was thus 72 per cent. by sea, against 28 per cent. by land, precisely the average of the 5 previous years. In 1847 the proportion was relatively 74 and 26; so that it appears the maritime commerce in 1848 did not maintain the increase of 2 per cent. to which it attained in the previous year. This variation, however, is almost entirely confined to the import trade; the imports by sea in 1847 amounting to 72 per cent., and in 1848 to only 67 per cent. With respect to the exports, they were proportioned as in the preceding year, viz, 75 per cent. by sea, and 25 per cent. by land.—We have seen that the total amount of the maritime commerce of F. in 1848 was 1,441,000,000 f. official value; of this amount, vessels sailing under the national flag took 712,000,000 f., or 49 per cent., and foreign vessels 729,000,000 f., or 51 per cent. The proportion in 1847 was respectively 46 and 54 per cent., which was also the average of the 5 preceding years. As compared with 1847 the French mercantile navy had decreased 19 per cent., and 11 per cent. on the 5 years' average. In the number and tonnage of foreign vessels trading with French ports the decrease was more marked, amounting to 30 per cent. on 1847, and 21 per cent. on the 5 years' average. The following will show more clearly the proportion existing between French and foreign vessels engaged in trade with F .: 36 per cent. (In 1847, . Average of 5 previous years, 37 ... Average of 5 previous years, 63 Foreign vessels (In 1848, The French tonnage consequently increased in 1848, as compared with the foreign, in the proportion of 6 and 5 per cent. On reviewing the general trade of F. with its colonies and foreign powers, the order in which they stand, ranking them according to the importance of the trade with each, is thus: England, the United States, Switzerland, Belgium, the Sardinian states, Spain, the German Zollverein, Russia, Turkey. These countries absorbed 71 per cent. of the entire trade. Brazil, Holland, and the Two Sicilies are next in importance, and took 5 per cent. in the aggregate. With respect to the French colonies, Algeria took about 41 per cent., thus occupying the 7th place in the order of importance; and the trade with all the other colonial possessions together amounted to only 5 per cent. The residue, amounting to rather more than a 7th of the whole, was distributed in minor proportions among 35 various states.—The trade with the following states decreased in 1848, as compared with the previous year: United States, 16 per cent.; Sardinia, 16 per cent.; Belgium, 24 per cent.; Spain, 21 per cent.; the German Zollverein, 39 per cent.; Russia, 56 per cent.; Turkey, 57 per cent. The trade with England, on the other hand, increased about 11,000,000 f., or 4 per cent.; and that with Switzerland rather more than 4,000,000 f. With the four colonies of Reunion, Cayenne, Guadaloupe, and Martinique, the total commerce presents a falling off of 44 per cent.; and with Algeria of 16 per cent. In 1847 the commerce of F. with the United States amounted in value to 223,000,000 f., and that with Great Britain to 189,000,000 f.; in 1848 the former had decreased to 156,000,000 f., or 30 per cent. less, whilst the trade with England had increased 12 per cent., amounting to 212,000,000 f., official value. On analyzing the proportionate value of the imports into F. from various countries during 1848 we obtain the following results: The value of produce received in France from the United States was 127,000,000 f., against 160,000,000 f. in 1847; decrease, 20 per cent. From Switzerland the amount was 105,000,000 against 106 in 1847. Belgium comes third in order, with 93,000,000 against 150 in 1847; decrease, 38 per cent. The arrivals from Sardinia, which had declined to 90,000,000 in 1847, amounted in 1848 to only 68,000,000. Those from England present a striking decrease, amounting to only 56,000,000, against 109,000,000 in 1847; whilst the average of the 5 preceding years was not less than 135,000,000. Russia, which sent, in 1847, 111,000,000 of produce, in 1848 sent only 41,000,000, or 63 per cent. less; it must, however, be remarked, that the decrease is principally exhibited in grain, of which the export was 49,000,000 less in the year under review. The German Zollverein sent into F. 40,000,000 f. of produce against 70,000,000 f. in 1847; bing a decrease of 42 per cent. Next comes Spain, which furnished 32,000,000 f. against 50,000,000 f. in 1847. The imports from Turkey, in 1847, amounted to 98,000,000 f., inclusive of 55,000,000 f. of grain; in 1848 the total imports from that quarter were only 25,000,000. From the British East Indies came 21,000,000 f., against 33,000,000 f. in 1847; from the Netherlands 19,000,000 f., against 25,000,000 f. in 1847. From the above eleven countries came 73 per cent. of the total imports. The amount of 8 per cent. was furnished, in the aggregate, by the following French possessions: the islands of Reunion, Guadaloupe, Martinique, St. Pierre, and Miquelon, the produce of the French fisheries, the French possessions in India, Senegal, Algeria, and French Guyana; this proportion was about the same as in the preceding year. The remaining 19 per cent. of the imports was furnished in smaller proportions by various other countries; among which may be mentioned Chili, from which country the imports have steadily increased since 1844; those from China, Cochin-China, and Oceania have tripled in value during the last 5 years. Commenting on the above returns, the Constitutionnel says, "Many serious lessons may be derived from the general table of the commerce of F. in the year 1848. They show how dearly the workingclasses have to pay for the luxury of revolutions and the enjoyment of revolutionary government. How many privations, how much distress of all sorts, do these long columns of figures reveal! The general commerce of F., which embraces all our intercourse with foreign countries, whatever be the destination of the goods imported or the origin of those exported, turned, in 1848, on an official value of 2,015,000,000 f., being a decrease of 599,000,000 f., or 23 per cent., on the results of the preceding year! As regards the special commerce, which only concerns the merchandise imported for our consumption and the national goods exported-a trade which more directly interests us-the decrease is still greater; the special commerce of 1848 amounted to 1,390,000,000 f., which exhibits a falling off, as compared with 1847, of 26 per cent. The sum of 1,390,000,000 f., which represents the total amount of the special commerce, is thus divided: Imports, 556,000,000 f.; and exports, 834,000,000 f. The difference, as compared with 1847, is 43 per cent. as regards the imports, and 6 per cent. as regards the exports. This decline of 43 per cent. (or nearly one moiety of the imports) attests the sufferings experienced by our manufacturing industry. It is known, in fact, that the goods imported chiefly consist of raw materials which we manufacture in F. Thus, for example, the imports of silk have fallen from 76,000,000 f. to 38,000,000 f. only; woollen goods, from 30,000,000 f. to 13,000,000 f.; oleaginous seeds, from 34,000,000 f. to 11,000,000 f.; undressed skins, from 25,000,000 f. to 16,000,000 f.; unwrought iron, from 14,000,000 f. to 6,000,000 f.; zinc, from 5,000,000 f. to 2,000,000 f. The consumption of colonial sugar has declined from 55,000,000 f. to 30,000,000 f. It would be impossible to calculate all the losses which have been consequent on the suspension of the industrial movement-losses to the manufacturer, whose materiel remained unproductive, and losses to the operative, who barely worked one day out of two. The exports have sustained a reduction far less considerable than the imports. The difference, as we have shown, is only tured produce, and, on the other, by the sacrifices which our manufacturers imposed on themselves in order to make an outlet for the goods which incumbered their magazines. Our exchanges have declined-viz., by 16 per cent. with the United States of America, 16 with Sardinia, 21 with Spain, 24 with Belgium, 30 with the German Zollverein, &c. The only increase is noticeable in our trade with England and the Swiss republic. Our colonial trade has fallen off by 44 per cent. Our transit trade has not suffered less. The carrying of foreign produce across the French territory amounted to a weight of 351,000 quintals, against 650,000 in 1847-equivalent to a decline of 46 per cent. As to the maritime movement of France with the colonies and with foreign countries, it has declined from 3,146,000 to 1,995,000 tons—equivalent to 27 per cent. The foreign flag, however, has suffered to a greater extent than the French. In concluding this sad enumeration, we may state that the produce of the customs' duties was in 1848 less by 48,000,000 f. than in 1847. Our colonial sugar figures for 19,000,000 f.; woollens, for 4,000,000 f.; unwrought iron, for 3,000,000 f.; coffee, for 2,000,000 f.; olive oil, for 2,000,000 f., &c. In Paris alone the falling off of the customs' receipts was nearly 50 per cent." In 1823 it was estimated that 100,000 kilogrammes of goods were thus introduced into France; -in 1825, 187,315; -in 1826, 2,100,000 kilogrammes; all these estimates being reported as rather under the mark. The calculation was made at 21 kilogrammes, or 11 lbs., as a pro rata per dog. These dogs sometimes carry 10 kilogrammes, and sometimes even 12. The above estimate supposes that one dog in 10, in certain districts, and in others, one in twenty, was killed: but in the opinion of many of the custom officers not more than one in 75 is destroyed, even when notice has been given, and the dogs are expected. Tobacco and colonial produce are generally the objects of this illicit trade; sometimes cotton-twist and manufactures. In the neighbourhood of Dunkirk, dogs have been taken with a burden of the value of 600, 800, or even 1,200 francs. Publications hostile to the government have not unfrequently been so introduced. The dogs which are trained to these 'dishonest habits' are conducted in packs to the foreign frontier; they are kept without food for many hours; they are then beaten and laden, and at the beginning of the night started on their travels. They reach the abodes of their masters, which are generally selected at two or three leagues from the frontiers, as speedily as they can, where they are sure to be well-treated and provided with a quantity of food. These results, compared with those of 1843, showed an increase of 330 ships, and 51,000 tons. The total movement, amounting to 3,288,000 tons, might be classified in three parts: the first, including the navigation in competition with foreign countries, employing 2,803,000 tons; the second, comprising the reserved navigation with the colonies and other provinces of F., amounting to 371,000 tons; the third, representing the great fisheries, and encouraged by premiums, amounted to 114,000 tons. The following was the navigation of F. to and from foreign countries in 1847, 1848, and 1849: Entered inwards. French ships. Tonnage. "Previous to the wars of the Revolution," says a French journal, "the effective of the commercial naval service of F. was 500,000 tons. According to the customs' report, it increased to 604,000 for 1844. This augmentation, however, is not proportioned to the development of the industrial activity. It appears yet more moderate when compared with the enormous increase of the marines of our rivals. In 1788 the tonnage of the commercial navy of England was 1,120,000 tons; at present it is 3,000,000. The United States in 1841 had 2,130,000 tons. Thus it will be seen that our effective is but the sixth of that of Great Britain, and the quarter of that of the United States. During the last five years the loss in our merchant-service has been considerable, since its tonnage in 1844 showed a decrease of 69,000 tons when compared with 1839. In 1839 F. possessed 15,600 vessels; she now has but 13,679. Of this number 8,900 are under 30 tons; 2,852 from 30 to 99; and among the 1,708 which are described as vessels of from 99 to 300 tons, one-quarter at most are fit to make long voyages. Add to this number 219 large vessels of from 300 to 600 tons, and we shall have about 652 large vessels capable of carrying heavy merchandise and performing long voyages. We do not possess a single ship of 700 tons, whilst England sends to her colonies whole fleets of ships each of 1,200 tons or more. In 1836 F. had 861 ships of from 200 to 800 tons; in 1840, 726; in 1841, 658; in 1843, 655; and at the close of 1844 we had but 652 of from 200 to 600 tons. Thus our privateers have drawn from our commerce, in the course of eight years, 209 of our large vessels; those of less tonnage have undergone the same process. The official documents of 1840 show that the general increase was 1,007 ships of 46 tons upon an average, and that the loss was 1,153 vessels, showing a figure of 146 on the side of the losses. Bordeaux, which in 1839 possessed 411 ships, had only 399 in 1840; III. * and in 1841 only 307 of a tonnage of 63,875 tons. Banks.] An account recently published of the operations and circulation of those French banks, or branches, which enjoy the privilege of issuing notes payable at sight and to bearer, is of some interest, as affording the means of comparison with the bank-paper of a similar description issued by the provincial banks, the bank of England, and its branches in this country. The average circulation of the bank of F. was stated at 210,000,000 francs in 1838; and of its four branches at Rheims, St. Etienne, St. Quentin, and Montpellier, at 1,932,000 f.; which together might be taken as the total mean circulation of bank of F. paper in 1838. In 1843 the maximum was 247,000,000 f., and the minimum 216,000,000 f.; whereas, in 1844, the maximum was 271,000,000 f., and the minimum 233,000,000 f. The medium circulation in 1843 was 230,000,000 f.; in 1844 it was 248,000,000 f. Any advantage to the bank, however, from this source is only apparent, as the increase in the amount of bullion fully kept pace with the above issue of notes. In 1843 the maximum stock held was maximum 279,000,000 f, minimum 234,000,000 f., medium 256,000,000 f. The report incidentally remarks, that this maximum has only been surpassed twice-namely, in 1832 and 1838; ness to the extent of 78,000,000 f.; the total operations in 1843 having been 243,000,000 f., giving a nett profit of 1,160,000 f.; in 1844 they were 321,000,000 f., yielding nett 1,420,000 f. The profit derived from the branch-establishments was estimated at 20 f. and 91 cents per share to the proprietors. The bank of Bordeaux-one of those privileged by charter-had, in 1838, an average circulation of 12,755,000 f.; the bank of Lyons, 8,288,000 f.; the bank of Marseilles, 5,873,500 f.; the bank of Rouen, 5,483,000 f.; and the three smaller banks of Havre, Nantes, and Lille, from 785,000 f. to 2,443,000 f. each. The whole amount of the bank-note circulation of F., corresponding to that of this country, appears to have been, in 1838, 248,968,000 f., or something less than £10,000,000. It must be understood, however, that this account did not extend to the paper-issues of the Laffitte and other joint-stock banks, which were not empowered to issue notes payable on demand and to bearer, although they contrived to evade the letter of the law by creating a considerable paper circulation in all essential points the same, -the mere difference being that the paper so issued professes to be drawnlike bills of exchange-at three or more days' sight, although really circulating and meant to remain in circulation like other bank-note paper payable on demand. The average discounts of the bank of F. were given in 1838 at 801,000,000 fr; and of its four branches at about 83,000,000 fr. The average discounts of the bank of Bordeaux for this same , ar were stated at 126,722,000 fr.; and those of the six other banks at about 225,000,000 fr The average discounts of the whole were stated at 1,235,777,000 fr. The average amount of the assets, for the same term, were thus noted: For the bank of F., 117,000,000 fr.; for its four branches, amounts varying from about 2,005,000 fr. to 4,500,000 fr.; for the bank of Bordeaux, 8,440,000 fr.; and for the five other banks, amounts ranging respectively from about 2,500,000 to 7,300,000 fr. The total average of all the banks thus amounted in 1840 to 165,325,000 fr. assets, against nearly 249,000,000 fr. of note circulation. The report of the operations of the bank of France for 1844, gives the following statement of the results, and of the position of the establishment at that date. "Although the past year shows an increase in the amount of business when compared with the preceding one, yet the profits of the institution have again suffered a diminution, and the dividends have in consequence been decreased. The dividends in 1842 amounted to 136 fr. per share; in 1843, they were 122 fr. per share; in 1844, they were only 107 fr. per share. In explanation of this decrease in the profits, the report points out that the increase in the total operations was wholly formed by the increase of 78,000,000 fr. in the business of the branch-banks, which are the least profitable part; while against this there must be placed the falling-off of about 30,000,000 fr. in the total operations of the Paris establishment; this was principally made up by the balance between the decrease of 22,182,217 fr. in the amount of discounts,-of a decrease of 18,672,100 fr. in the amount of advances on stocks. and an increase of 11,081,400 fr. in the advances on bullion. Averages. Books. Balances. Fr. Ct. Fr. Ct. Canals.] M. Chevalier, in the first volume of his great work Des Interets en France, published in 1838, considering France in a hydrographic point of view, represents it as consisting of seven basins:- 1. The basin of the Rhone, inclining towards the S, and penetrating to the eastern mountains of Jura and the Vosges; and of which the whole of F. towards the Mediterranean is but the complement. 2. The basin of the Rhine, inclining to the N, which—though of small importance, considering the quantity of French territory it occupies-is of the which forms nearly the whole of central F. The Loire has most important branches, some of which are, and others of which may become navigable. The principal of these are the Vienne, the Cher, the Allier to the left; the Mayenne, with its two branches of the Sarthe and the Loir, to the right. 5. The basin of the Seine, which inclines to the NE, and which is distinguished by its high state of cultivation, its industrious pop., and the absence of mountains from its soil. Considering the size of its basin, no river has a larger number of branches than the Seine, of which the Aube, the Yonne, and the Marne, are important. 6 and 7. The basins of the Scheldt and the Meuse, generally inclining to the NE, and to which are attached the basins of the Somme and the Aa. These embrace provs. distinguished for pop., manufacture, and mines. Henry IV. made the canal of Briare, which unites the Seine and the Loire, or Paris and the sea.—The Canal-du-Midi was designed by Louis to connect the ocean with the Mediterranean, and to open a communication between the Rhone and the Garonne. This canal is 64 French leagues, or 180 m. long; 144 ft. broad, including the tow-paths; and 6 ft. deep. At Narouse, the highest point between the two seas, is a basin 1,200 ft. in length, and 900 ft. in breadth, with 7 ft. depth of water. The water of this basin is conveyed by one sluice towards the Atlantic, and by another towards the Mediterranean. To keep a constant supply of water for this basin, another reservoir was formed at St. Feriole, 7,200 ft. long, 3,000 ft. broad, and 90 ft. deep, two sides of which are formed by two mountains, and the third by a large and strong mole, through which runs an aqueduct to the other basin. There are 60 sluices or locks on the line, 15 of which are towards the Atlantoises deep, with horse-ways on both sides. There are 26 falls on this canal, the most considerable of which is that near Beziers, at the end of a reach 30 m. long. About 12,000 men were employed 15 years in making this canal; the expense was £1,600,000; and it costs above £12,000 annually to keep it in repair. It falls into the Garonne 1 a m. below Toulouse; but the navigation of the river is so bad till its junction with the Tarn, that the boats upon it are unable to carry any depth of lading.—The conception of this work was followed by the Canal d'Orleans, connecting the Loire and the Seine.—The Canal de Bourgogne, from the Rhone to the Seine, was made in 1759, and an union between the former river and the Loire was formed in 1784 by the Canaldu-Centre; while, in the same year, the Seine and Loire were connected, through the medium of the Yonne, by the canal of Nivernois.—Napoleon made the canal of St. Quentin from the Scheldt to the Seine, and commenced a communication between the Rhone and the Rhine, as well as another between Nantes and Brest.—The canals just enumerated are nearly all in a state of completion, but much is still wanting to perfect the interior navigation of F. The basin of the Rhone is attached to the Middle Loire by the Canal-du-Centre, 73 m. long, and also to the Seine and the Rhine, while the railroad by St. Etienne connects it with the Upper Loire; however, there is a want of communication with the Lower Rhine, as there is no permanent navigation on the Rhine as far as Strasburg, but only to Manheim. Again, it is impossible to proceed to the Lower Scheldt and the Meuse without taking a roundabout course by Paris. There are two considerable gaps in the course of navigation, but they would be removed by a work of moderate labour,—the cutting of a canal between the Saone and the Marne, which would at once establish a communication between the Rhone and the Moselle, and the Meuse, the former of which is connected with the Lower Rhine, and the latter with the Lower Scheldt, the Canal-dela-Sambre, and the Canal-Charleroi leading to Brussels. A canal from the Marne to the Aisne, by Rheims, would be of considerable utility in opening a communication of the Rhone with those places which lie between the Lower Seine and the Meuse, and by another canal, from the Aisne to the Oise, this communication would be carried on so as to facilitate the passage from Marseilles to the Northern sea, Amiens, Arras, Lille, and Dunkirk. The Rhone, notwithstanding its proximity to the Garonne, is nearly cut-off from all connection with that river, except by the Canal-du-Midi, to which the Canalde-Beaucair serves as a continuation. But these canals offer no communication with Bordeaux, not being continued nearly so far; and if they did, still an immense part of the basin of the Garonne would remain insulated. The basin of the Seine is wellprovided. The canals of Orleans, Nivernois, and Briare, all converge towards Paris, and connect it sufficiently with the Loire; while the canal of St. Quentin connects it with the Scheldt and the Somme canal. Again, the canals of the Sambre and of Ardennes open a communication with the Meuse. The basin of the Loire must evidently be the main source of all interior navigation, and across its surface must be the communication between Northern and Southern France. A grand desideratum for the outward navigation is a connection with the Danube, which would give F. a line of commerce as far as the Black sea, and the very position of the French rivers seems to point out such an union. The inland navigation of F. is composed of 8,255 kilometres of rivers, and 3,699 kil. of canals,-in all, 11,954 kil., or about 7,429 m. Seventeen deps. are entirely destitute of inland navigation. Amongst these are the level plains of the Orne, the Indre, and the dep. of Eureet-Loire. Roads.] There are in F. 630 roads of the first class, called Routes royales, of which only 15,000 m. out of 21,800 m., their total length, are in a state of repair. They are from 43 to 65 ft. wide. One of the causes of their imperfect condition arises | The duration of the lease, and the tariff of changes from the cumbersome system under which they are managed. Between 1830 and 1847, we find that there were grants to the amount of 266,000,000 f., or £10,640,000, for repairs and improvements of various kinds connected with roads; and that other special and exceptional grants were made, in order, among other things, to give employment to the working class, or to remedy disasters occasioned by inundations. The number of bridges coustructed by the state, or by companies, on the Routes royales, and on the departmental, strategic, and country-roads, amounts to 481. The Rhone, the Durance, the Dordogne, the Garonne, the Loire, the Saone, and the Seine, are crossed by some of the finest among them. The money appropriated for this purpose alone amounts to about 24,000,000 f., or nearly £1,000,000. In the course of 16 years, previous to 1830, 57 bridges were constructed by companies; and from 1830 to 1847, as many as 162.—The Routes departementales, or second class of roads, are 1,381 in number, and their total length is 22,733 m. They are kept in repair by the respective deps., and are, generally speaking, in a very unsatisfactory state. These two classes of roads resemble our mail-coach and cross-roads. The parish-roads, or Routes vicinales, are 468,527 in number; total length 479,464 m.; average length of each about 1 m. The expense of keeping them in repair devolves upon each commune: they resemble the lanes and by-roads of England. The length of the above three classes of roads is nearly 525,000 m. Railways.] Before 1825 there did not exist in F. a railway destined for the general service of the public. Between 1825 and 1828 M. Beaunier constructed one from St. Etienne to the Loire. It was a single line, 21 kil. in length. The second railway constructed in F. was that from St. Etienne to Lyons. This was a double line, 60 kil. in length, and was opened throughout at the end of 1832. The third railway constructed was from Andrezieux to Roanne, and forms, with the two preceding undertakings, an uninterrupted line from Lyons to Roanne. It was a single line, 67 kil. in length. The small branch undertaking from Montrond to Montbrison, only 16 kil. in length, was made in 1835. In the month of October, in the same year, was opened, in the deps. of the Cote-d'Or and Saone-et-Loire, the small line from Epinac to the Bourgogne canal, which was at first exclusively devoted to the carriage of oil in waggons drawn by horses, but was afterwards authorized to carry passengers. The length of this railway was 28 kil. The total length of these five undertakings was altogether 192 kil., or 119 m., and formed the whole of the railway accommodation which F. possessed before the year 1837. The sixth line of railway, opened in 1837, was that from Paris to St. Germain, 20 kil. in length. In 1839 the following three lines were completed and opened:-Montpellier to Cette, 27 kil. in length; Versailles, r. bank, 19 kil. in length, exclusive of the part connected with the St. Germain line; and the Denain and Abscon, 14 kil. in length. In 1840 were opened the Mulhouse and Thann railway, 19 kil. in length; the Gard and the Grand- Combe, 86 kil. in length; and the Versailles, l. bank, 18 kil. in length. In 1841 the first line opened was that from Bordeaux to La Teste-de-Buch, 52 kil. in length. The next line opened was the Strasburg and Basle railway, described as the first great undertaking completed, being 140 kil. long. The same year the branch from Paris to Corbeil was opened, and the length of the line was 32 kil. Thus, in 1841 the 224 kil. of railway were increased to 619 kil., or 384 m., perfectly complete and in operation. In the session of 1842, a plan was definitively sanctioned by the chambers for the construction of a comprehensive system of railway communication, by bringing the funds of the government to the aid of private enterprise. The outline of the plan was this: -The government to provide the land, and construct the earth-work, bridges, stations, &c., leaving it to the company to lay down the rails or permanent way, and to provide the carrying establishment. For this the company obtain the privilege of working the railway for a given number of years, at the expiration of which period the government have the option of purchasing the whole stock, &c., at a valuation. &c., are settled by arbitration, in each case, before the concession is made to the company. During the session of 1842, however, little progress was made, although several companies were in the field to undertake the principal lines. This was owing to difficulties which arose in the chamber-of-deputies with regard to the details of the arrangements proposed; but, in the meantime, the engineering works on the line from Paris to the Belgian frontier, and other important lines, were actively prosecuted by the government. In 1842 only the two small trunks from Lille and Versailles to the Belgian frontier were opened, in length about 28 kil. In 1843 two important lines were brought into operation-first, the Paris and Orleans railway, 124 kil. long, without taking into account the Corbeil branch; and second, the Paris and Rouen, 137 kil. long. By the completion of the works opened this year, the extent of railway communication in F. had increased to 905 kil. or 561 m. The year 1844 passed without any line being opened. In 1845 the Montpellier and Nismes railway was opened, and its 50 kil. form a continuation with the Montpellier and Cette and Gard lines. Up to the end of this year F. possessed 955 kil. of railway. In the following year, 1846, this extent was increased 434 kil. by the opening of other undertakings. First among these was the Orleans and Tours, 115 kil. in length; the next was the small line from Paris to Sceaux, 11 kil. in length; and lastly, the Great Northern of France, 308 kil. in length. Hence the number of railways opened for traffic in France before the 1st of January, 1847, was 22, and their total length 1,389 kil., or nearly 861 m. The Paris and Rouen railroad was extended to Havre, a distance of 57 m. in 1849, placing Paris in direct communication with the extensive docks now built or building at that port. The Paris and Orleans railroad has been extended in several directions: 1st. by the Orleans, Tours, and Bordeaux railway, 300 m. long, now in progress; 2d. by the Tours and Nantes railway, 120 m. in length; and 3d. by the central railway from Orleans to Vierzon, Bourges, and Chateauroux. Of the grand trunkline from Paris to the Mediterranean, a considerable part of the section from Paris to Lyons, 300 m. in length, has been completed. It is to be continued from Lyons, through Avignon, to Marseilles; and the portion from Marseilles to Avignon is now in operation. A branch line is to run from Tarascon on the trunk line, to Nismes, Montpellier, and Cetté. The line from Paris to Strasburg, the length of been nearly completed; and railway communication nection with Brussels, Holland, the north of Europe, Saône, Saumur to Angers, Versailles to Chartres, Noyon to Chauny, Saint Pierre to Calais: total 574 burg to Sarreburg; Tonnerre to Dijon; Tarascon to Beaucaire; Tours to Poitiers; Angers to Nantes; and probably Chartres to La Loupe: total 513 kil. All the French railways, with one or two exceptions, are only conceded to the companies who have undertaken their construction, for a term of years, so that the companies have to provide for the reimbursement of the capital at the expiration of the lease by means of a sinking-fund, varying according to the length to which it extends. The railways from Paris to Rouen and to Orleans were conceded directly for terms of 99 years; and that from Rouen to Havre for 97. After 1842 the government began to work on its own account on several of the great lines of railway; and since that period, the greater part of the new lines have been put up to auction, and conceded to the company offering the greatest reduction on a maximum term of concession fixed by the chambers. For some, the government has been reimbursed by the companies for any outlay expended on the line conceded to them; for others, it continues to pay all the expenses of the earth works, leaving the company only to provide the rails and working-stock-the length of lease of course varying, as one or other of these alternatives has been adopted. Among the lines in the latter position, are those from Orleans to Bordeaux, from Orleans to Vierzon, from Tours to Nantes, from Avignon to Marseilles, and from Paris to Strasburg; and in the former are the Northern railway, those from Boulogne to Amiens, from Paris to Lyons, and several other important undertakings. The length of the leases has varied much, according to the time at which the concession took place. The Boulogne and Amiens railway, conceded in October, 1844, before the excitement of 1845, was taken for 99 years; the Northern railway, taken during the heat of that excitement, has only a lease of 38 years: the Paris and Lyons, taken subsequently, has one of 41. The Lyons and Avignon is conceded for 45 years, the Bordeaux and Cette for 66. The Paris and Strasburg railway is conceded for 44 years, and that from Tours to Nantes for 34.—By their acts, the various railway companies have a certain maximum tariff imposed upon them, under which they may make any alteration, but which they cannot exceed. These rates are 1.66d. per mile for a first-class passenger; 1.24d. for a second-class; and 0.91d. for a third-class passenger. For goods, the maximum rates allowed vary on each line, and are generally much higher than those actually charged on lines already at work.—The following is a tabular view of the share capital of the various French railway companies:- Paid-up Capital Total share, Name of line. capital. not called. capital Andrésieux and Roanne, *** Avignon and Marseilles, Total. Or a little more than £49,000,000. Besides which, the several companies have raised by loan about £2,400,000. Weights and Measures.] During the republican CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CO government, a system of uniform weights and measures was established on a simple plan; the elementary measure being connected with the dimensions of the terraqueous globe. This measure, which is called metre, that is a measure par excellence, is the ten-millionth part of a quarter of the terrestrial meridian, that is, of the distance of the equator from the pole, and is equal to 3 feet 44 inches. The arc serves to measure the surface of the soil, in the same manner as the arpent; it is equal to 100 square métrés, or 948 feet 33 inches. The stéré is equal to a cubic métré, or 2000 feet cubic. The litré is the measure of capacity; it is equal to a cubic decimétré, or 5043 cubic inches, or 1-20th of the former pint of Paris. The grammé marks the weight; it is equal to the weight of a cubic centimétré of pure water at its maximum of density. It has been found equal to 18 1000 French grains, of which 5 1600 grains make 4725 English grains; and 489 558 grammé make a pound of the standard of the mint of Paris. These five primitive measures, viz., métrés, arcs, sterés, litrés, and grammes, are successively multiplied or divided by 10, in order to form the greater or smaller measures, analogous to the decimal system of arithmetic. The three divisors are deci, cento, and milli: expressing the 10th, 100th, and 1,000th part. Thus, decimétre is the 10th part of the métre, déciare the 10th part of the arc., &c. The four multiplicators are deca, hecta, kilio, and myria, denoting 10 times, 100 times, 1,000 times, and 10,000 times: thus the decimétré is 10 métrés; the hectare, 100 arcs; the kiliométré, 1,000 metres; the myriagrammé, 10,000 grammés. Monies.] Accounts were formerly kept in livres; the present mode of keeping accounts is in francs of 100 centimes each; 5 centimes = 1 sou; 20 sous = 1 franc. In hasty calculations it is customary to regard the franc as worth 10d. British currency; but the British sovereign is worth 25 francs, 20 centimes. The silver coins most in use are the piece of 5 francs, the piece of 3 francs, and the piece of 50 centimes. The gold coins most in use are the Napoleon or new Louis, worth 20 francs; the double Napoleon, worth 40 francs; and the old Louis, worth 23 francs, 50 centimes. Vital statistics.] The French empire, in 1812, including Holland and the German deps., previous to the invasion of Russia, had a pop. of 43,000,000 souls; besides 6,400,000 in the kingdom of Italy, and 1,500,000 in the Illyrian provinces. According to the official returns for 1827, the total pop. of the present French empire, including Corsica, was 31,851,545. The pop. of F., in 1840, was 34,136,695; in 1846, 35,401,761.—In respect of means and domestic condition, this pop. has been thus classified: 450,000 rich families, living in towns, though generally possessed of landed property; 600,000 families dependent on the state, but generally also possessed of property; 900,000 families without property, living in towns, by the exercise of industry; 800,000 families uncertain, small stockholders, pensioners, &c.; 2,810,000 urban families, in all about 11,240,000 souls. 4,800,000 country families, in all about . 24,000.000 ... 35,240,000 souls. According to the tables inserted in the Annuaire, published by the Bureau des Longitudes, the mean annual number of births in France, in the eight years between 1817 and 1824, was 961,145; that of deaths, 761,326; and that of marriages 225,658. According to Dupin, the pop. of F. is increasing at the rate of about 200,000 annually. The report of M. Moreau de Jonnes to the Academy of Sciences, in 1838, showed an increase, in 1836, of births above deaths of 208,120. The proportion of the births to the total pop. in that year was, therefore, as 1 to 34; of deaths, as 1 to 43; and of marriages, as 1 to 121. To find an absolute mortality so small as that of 1836, we must go back as far as 1824, the mortality since that year having always greatly exceeded that of 1836. As to the relative mortality of 1 in 43, it is the first time that this proportion existed in F., the least proportion that had before occurred was that of 1823, viz. 1 in 41.5. Since 1816, this proportion has varied from 1 in 38 to 1 in 40. M. de Jonnes reports that in 1846, when the pop. of F. was 35,500,000, the number of births was 983,475, being 1 for every 36 inhabitants. At nearly the same period (1840- 1841-1842) the number of births in Russia was in the proportion of 1 in 23 inhabitants; in Prussia, 1 in 27; in Austria, 1 in 26; in England, 1 in 34. But in 1782, when the great rights of property, the convents, and mortmain predominated in F., out of 25,500,000 souls there were 975,703 births, or nearly as many as in 1846; the proportion of births to the number of the inhabitants being then 1 in 25. The great question is, What was the mortality? During the last period of 8 years the mortality in F. was about 1 in 47; in 1781 it was 1 in 25 and 24. The rate of mortality has therefore diminished nearly one-half in 60 years. In 1846 the mortality in Russia was I in 28; in Austria, 1 in 33; in Prussia, 1 in 38; in England, 1 in 45. During the period between 1817 and 1841 the number of marriages in F. was 1 in 127; in 1846 it was 1 in 134, although the pop. had continued to increase, because the question lies not only in the births but in the mortality also. There are more marriages and more births in the towns than in the country, and yet pop. increases more rapidly in the country, because in the country the youth are freer from those temptations to precocious debauchery which in the towns too frequently vitiate the constitution of the parents. Out of 14 births, 13 are legitimate.—In the Annuaire de l'Economie et de la Statistique for 1850, we have an analysis of a recent work by M. Bouvard, in which some curious results are arrived at. According to this statistician, the mean duration of life in F. is 40 years, 10 months, 17 days. But this mean duration varies considerably in different departments. In the Hautes-Pyrenees, it is 54 years, 8 months, and 20 days; in Orne, 53 years, 8 months, 16 days; in the Basses-Pyrenees, 49 years, 10 months, 9 days; in Cantal, 48 years, 9 days; Moselle, 48 years, 3 months, 15 days; Vienne, 48 years, 1 month, 17 days; Ardenne, 48 years, 20 days; Haute-Marne, 47 years, 11 months, 13 days; Deux-Sèvres, 47 years, 10 months, 5 days; Ariège, 47 years, 7 months, 6 days. And in other departments only the following: In the Bouches-du- Rhone, 31 years, 1 month, 28 days; in Seine, 31 years, 8 months, 5 days; Finistère, 33 years, 3 months, 11 days; Ille-et-Vilaine, 33 years, 4 months, 13 days; Basses-Alpes, 33 years, 7 months, 24 days; Morbihan, 34 years, 9 months, 25 days; Cher, 34 years, 10 months, 24 days; Vaucluse, 34 years, 11 months, 27 days; Var, 35 years, 11 months, 27 days; Pyrenees- Orientales, 35 years, 2 months, 27 days. "In this nomenclature," the editors of the Annuaire remark, "there are not three departments which border upon one another; those of the High and Low Pyrenees, it is true, approach very nearly; but why is it that Orne, which is separated by 150 leagues from these two deps., is in a condition of longevity very nearly identical? Among the deps. in which the mean duration of life is in a very bad condition, those of Var and Vaucluse present the same result—34 years, 11 months, and 27 days. What reason can establish such an analogy?" After having made known the mean duration of existence, M. Bouvard also indicates the number of births by 10,000 individuals in each dep. The numbers are: In Finistère, 376; Loire- Inferieure, 376; Var, 375; Herault, 369; Loire, 369; Bas-Rhin, 355; Cher, 533; Ain, 352; Seine, 347; Vaucluse, 347. While in other deps. the numbers are only as follow:-Dordogne, 209; Jura, 210; Calvados, 219; Indre, 226; Eure, 231; Lot-et-Garonne, 234; Manche, 237; Gers, 240; Tarn-et-Garonne, 253; Maine-et-Loire, 256. The births are much more considerable in the deps. where the duration of life is most short. Thus we find Cher, Finistère, Seine, Var, and Vaucluse among the deps. which have the greatest number of children, and in which human life is the least long. The dep. of the Moselle doubles the number of its inhabitants in 68 years; Bas- Rhin, in 68 years; Haute-Loire, 74 years; Vosges, 76 years; Vienne, 78 years; Ardèche, 79 years; Ariège, 79 years; Corse, 82 years; Gard, 83 years. "It astonishes us," say the editors of the Annuaire, "that in this nomenclature we do not find any of the deps. in which life endures the longest; the Bas- Rhin is the only one in this category among the deps. which have the greatest number of births. If the pop. can double itself in so short a time in the deps. above cited, it takes a considerable time in other deps. to arrive at the same result. In the dep. of the Eure, it takes 1,750 years; in Var, 976 years; Calvados, 925 years; Ille-et-Vilaine, 630 years; Seine-et-Oise, 533 years; Bouches-du-Rhone, 425 years; Lot-et-Garonne, 358 years; Charente-Inferieure, 353 years; Manche, 298 years; Maine-et-Loire, 284 years. The general results, according to M. Bouvard, are: that the mean duration of life in F. is 40 years, 10 months, and 17 days; the mean number of births, 304 in 10,000 individuals; the mean period in which the pop. doubles itself, 137 years; the duration of life is 54 years in Hautes-Pyrénées, and only 31 years in the Bouches-du-Rhone; they number 376 births in Finistère, and only 209 in Dordogne; the dep. of the Moselle can double its pop. 25 times within the same period that that of the Eure doubles its pop. only once."-From official returns it appears that the number of foundlings in F., from 1815 to 1841 inclusive, amounted to 849,612, or, on an average, to 31,467 per annum. The deaths during the same period were 509,922 in number, or 18,886 yearly; and the average expense incurred by the state on account of the foundlings, from 1824 to 1841 inclusive, was 9,084,243 f.—The number of persons admitted into the hospitals of F. from the 1st of January, 1833, to the 31st of December, 1841, amounted during those eight years to 4,435,811, or 492,868 per annum; 362,951 died, and 3,546,233 recovered or left the hospitals for some other motive. The receipts of the hospitals in that interval were 509,644,330 f., and the expenditure 402,668,103 f., or, on an average, 90 f. 78 c. per patient. On the 1st of January, 1842, there were in France 1,230 hospitals, maintained at an expense of upwards of 56,000,000 f. a year; those of Paris alone cost nearly 14,000,000 f. The number of insane existing in the kingdom in 1841 was 18,367. In 1841, there were in F. 7,482 charitable boards, and the poor relieved by them (806,970) at a cost of 10,857,281 f. The pop. of Sweden amounts to about 3,500,000 souls, and has only 3 mendicants in every 400 persons, while in Norway they reckon 5 out of every 100; in Denmark, 4; in Wurtemberg, 5; in Switzerland, 10; in Italy, 13; in France, 15; and in the British islands collectively, 17, although in England separately there are only 10. It appears from the calculation of M. C. Dupin, that a singular diminution in the height of the French has taken place since the Revolution. According to returns presented to the Chambers in 1826, it seems, that out of 1,033,422 youths, who appeared before the councils of revision, there were 380,213 rejected, because they did not even reach the low stature of 4 ft. 10 inches, or 5 ft. 14 in. English. This curious fact led him to examine into the causes of this diminution in size of the French race, which he states to be chiefly the wars of the Revolution, which cut down more particularly the virile part of the pop., and also the inferior food of the working-classes. The extreme repugnance which these classes yet manifest to vaccination, the imprudent liberality with which hospitals for foundlings have been endowed, and the numerous facilities of support afforded to poor or seduced mothers, have also appeared to M. Dupin positive causes of the enfeebling of the present generations. A glance at the table already inserted will show that the comparative pop. of the French deps. corresponds to the comparative industry of the districts. Thus, omitting the cap., the deps. of Nord, Rhône, Haut and Bas-Rhin, and Seine-Inférieure, are at once the most populous and the most industrious; while those of Basses and Hautes-Alpes, Landes, and Lozère, are, on the contrary, the most thinly inhabited, and the least productive. More recently, M. Raudot informs us that from 1839 to 1845 there were on an average 37,326 recruits a-year fit for service, of less stature than 5 ft. 1 in. French; therefore, if the ancient stature was required, it would have been necessary to send away as improper for service one-half of the men called on to perform their turn of duty. In the seven classes called out from 1839 to 1845 there were 491,000 men exempted, and only 486,000 declared fit for service. In the 17 classes from 1831 to 1837 there had been only 459,000 exempted, and 504,000 declared fit for service. Criminal Statistics.] In 1826, the number of persons charged with criminal offences in F. was 7,591; of whom 603, who fled. were condemned par contumace; of the remainder, 2,640 were acquitted, and 4,348 found guilty. Of the latter, 150 were sentenced to death; 281 to hard labour for life; 1,139 to hard labour for various terms, and 1,228 to solitary imprisonment. The proportion of female criminals to males was about 20 in 100; and above half the accused persons were under 30 years of age. In England, the number of persons found guilty of criminal offences was 11,095, of whom 1,200 were condemned to death! In relation to the pop. of the kingdom, the number of offenders in F., in 1837, presented a proportion of 1 in every 4,144 inhabitants; in 1836 this proportion was 1 in 4,633. Some curious results have lately been presented to the Academy of Sciences, upon the progress of crime. It has been found that the mean annual progress of crime, from 1825 to 1838, was 79 on 2,099 (annual mean) accused of crime against persons. 894 against property other than robberies. 25 572 15,939 simple or qualified robberies. 1,237 38,540 condemned of any offences except simple robberies and forest-crimes (delits forestiers). 2,030 suicides. 74 Thus giving a total of 1,983 on 59,499 crimes, offences, or suicides, or about 2,000 on 60,000, or 1-30th of the annual mean.—From a series of tables of the criminal statistics of F., extending from 1826 to 1845, and divided into periods of 5 years, besides the two years which commenced the course, it appears that the mean number of accused for the first period, or from 1826 to 1830, was 7,130; for the second period, 1831 to 1835, 7,466; for the third period, 1836 to 1840, 7,885; for the fourth period, 1841 to 1845, 7,106. The figures for the two intermediate periods, it will be seen, are higher than those for the two others; and the figures of the last are lower than of the first; but the proportions are not the same, if we consider the accused according as they have committed crimes against persons or against property. The first period contains the lowest number of crimes against property, only 1,824 per annum on an average; the second period offers the greatest number of these, 2,371; while the third and fourth have only produced 2,153 and 2,220 respectively. But if we deduct from the number accused of crimes against the person during the second period, 1831 to 1835, about 1,000 accused of political offences, we shall have for the second period an average number of 2,170 accused of crimes against the person,—a number which differs very little from those in the third and fourth periods. With respect to persons accused of crimes against property, their number is lowest in the period 1841 to 1845, the average per annum being 4,918. After it comes the second period, which counts 5,095; the first, 5,306; the third, 5,732. Thus the difference between the third and the fourth period is 814 accused, that is to say, from 1841 to 1845, the mean annual number of persons accused of crimes against property was less, compared with the preceding five years, by more than 14 per cent. What is the corollary to be drawn from these figures? inquires a native journalist. Is it true, as is often asserted, that the number of crimes increases year by year in a frightful manner? Such is not the teaching of statistics: on the contrary, this science attests that from 1826 to 1847, the number of crimes properly so called, and qualified as such by the penal code, has not increased. If we leave out the exceptional year 1847, it has even diminished; and still more remarkable, it has diminished, although the pop. of F. has augmented during that period by more than one-tenth. It is true that by the side of this result statistics place another of a less satisfactory character; it is, that while certain classes of crimes have remained stationary or diminished since 1826, there are others which have augmented in considerable proportion. Thus the number of forgeries, which in the period 1826-1830 was only 403 per annum, has progressively increased, and in 1847 attained the height of 682. Fraudulent bankruptcies, which from 1826 to 1830 did not exceed an average of 99, rose in 1846 to 170, falling to 146 in 1847. In the same lapse of time the crime of coining has increased to a fourfold extent. A sad progression is likewise to be noticed in the excesses resulting from disordered passions. Violations and assaults with intent on adults have increased from 166 to 227 in 20 years. The same crimes committed on persons of tender years have progressed from an average of 139 to 359. The number for 1847 was 384. Hitherto we have spoken only of acts qualified as crimes by the penal law, and submitted as such to the courts of assize. As to those which are classed under the term offences, and as such sent to the police-courts, they have followed, since 1846, the ascending movement. These offences are divided into two classes, according as they come before the court on the complaint of the public authorities, or as mere civil cases. Charges of the first class are more serious, since they have for their object the repression of conduct which attacks the public morals, or social order. The others affect only private interest, and those but feebly. But during the twenty-two years embraced by statistics, we find that the number of persons tried on the demand of the minister has more than doubled. This number, which in 1826 was only 47,443, has gradually ascended to 124,159, the figures for 1847. It is to be noticed that the cases for swindling, abuse of confidence, and simple bankruptcy present an increase corresponding to that which we have remarked of the crimes of forgery, coining, and fraudulent bankruptcy." The investigated than in anterior years. In 1825 the number of offenders whose previous convictions could be ascertained was 11 per cent.; in 1835, 21 per cent. In 1842 the proportion had attained to 25 per cent.; at this figure it stood for three years; for correctional education of young convicts, 12; of agricultural colonies founded by government, 5; of the same governed by private individuals, 7; total, 145. On the 1st of Jan. 1848, the pop. amounted to 26,250 in the department prisons, 17,789 in the maisons centrales, and to 3,600 in the establishments of agricultural colonies for young convicts; total, 48,039. Races of the population.] The population of F. consists of French, Germans, Cimmerians, or, as they are also called, Bas-Bretons, Basques, and Italians; to whom may be added Jews, Gypsies, and Cagots. 1st. The French tribe amounts to about 25,500,000. They are derived from the Celts or Gauls, the Romans, and the Franks, but principally from the latter. Their language is the principal language of the country, and is generally understood and spoken by well-educated people throughout the whole of Europe. It has many and very different dialects. In appearance and character the French inhabitants of the different provinces, particularly those of the N and the S, differ considerably from one another. The French of the S are a much more ardent, irritable, and impetuous race than those of the N. That liveliness and cheerfulness for which the French are proverbially remarkable, are found in the most eminent degree in the Provençals, whose piercing black eyes, and highly animated features, are the faithful index of their character. 2d. The Bretons, or Bas-Bretons, amounting to about 920,000 in Lower Bretagne, are descended from the British Cimmerians, who, in the 5th cent., were driven from Great Britain by the Anglo-Saxons, and settled in this part of F., to which they gave their name. They still speak the ancient British or Cimmerian language, and are distinguished from the French, by their appearance, manners, customs, and dress. Their features indicate a phlegmatic disposition; but for honesty, benevolence, and hospitality, the true Bas-Breton is proverbial. It must be confessed, however, that superstition, ignorance, and a general dislike of innovation, are prominent features in their character, and that they must be regarded as some centuries behind the rest of their countrymen in civilization. They live mostly in hamlets or isolated farms. Their houses, or rather huts, are very dirty, and their agriculture is wretchedly bad; nevertheless the Breton is quite happy with his milk and oat-cakes. 3d. The Germans amount to nearly 3,000,000, being in part Walloous. The Germans inhabit Alsace, and a part of Lorraine. 4 They are very much attached to their own language, manners, and dress; but a great part of them speak the Lorraine patois, or the Roman-French dialect, and French is generally used in the towns. The German which is spoken in F. is extremely corrupt; and as it approaches the S becomes more and more similar to the Swiss dialects. In the middle parts of Alsace it is almost entirely the old Suabian of the middle ages. A multitude of old German words, now obsolete in the present German language, have been preserved here. The Walloons in the deps. of Nord and Pas-de- Calais speak Flemish; but in the towns French is almost everywhere predominant. 4th. The Basques, the descendants of the ancient Cantabrians, amount to about 108,000 individuals, chiefly located at the foot and in the valleys of the Pyrenees, where they have preserved their language and national dress. Honesty and valour are characteristic features of the Basques. The French dialect of Gascoigne is also intermixed with Basque words. 5th. The Italians, amounting to nearly 200,000 individuals, are chiefly confined to Corsica; a considerable number of them are Savoyards, who wander through the whole of France. The Corsicans speak Italian; but the language has been greatly corrupted by them, particularly in the mountains, where it is mixed with many Moorish expressions. 6th.
